metadata.title: The Fifth Book of Sentences
metadata.author: Taio of Zaragoza
known_issues: References to Antichrist and eschatological themes reflect medieval theological perspectives that may require contextual understanding for modern audiences.
The work contains extensive biblical citations that have been fully expanded per the translation guidelines.
Some passages contain dense theological argumentation that may be challenging for audio comprehension without visual reference to the Latin text.
Text is a compilation of excerpts from Gregory the Great's works, primarily the Moralia in Job, organized thematically by Taio
Some cross-references to 'Moral.' 'In Ezech.' and 'In Evang.' indicate source texts within Gregory's corpus
Translation assumes continuity between excerpts that may have been originally separated in their source contexts
Multiple citations reference specific chapters and sections of Gregory's works

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Book Five: Lovers of the World
chunks.latin: LIBER QUINTUS. CAPUT PRIMUM. De dilectoribus mundi.
chunks.english: Book Five. Chapter One: Lovers of the World
chunks.notes: This opens the fifth book of Taio's Sentences, a florilegium of Gregory the Great's writings

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omnes iniqui, dum corde transire ad aeterna negligunt, et cuncta praesentia, quia fugiunt, non intuentur, mentem in amore vitae praesentis figunt, et quasi longae habitationis in ea sibi fundamentum construunt, quia in terrenis rebus per desiderium solidantur.
chunks.english: All the wicked neglect to turn their hearts toward eternal things, and because they do not pay attention to how quickly present things pass away, they fix their minds on love of this present life. They build themselves a foundation as if for a long habitation here, because they are anchored in earthly things through their desires.
chunks.notes: Citation is from Gregory's Moralia, book sixteen, chapter ten

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Primus Cain civitatem construxisse describitur (Gen. IV), ut aperte monstretur quia ipse in terra fundamentum posuit, qui a soliditate aeternae patriae alienus fuit.
chunks.english: Cain is described as the first to build a city, as recorded in the book of Genesis, chapter four. This openly demonstrates that he laid his foundation on earth because he was alienated from the stability of the eternal homeland.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Peregrinus quippe a summis, fundamentum in imis posuit, qui intentionem cordis in terrena delectatione collocavit.
chunks.english: A person who is an exile from the heights lays his foundation in the depths when he places the intention of his heart in earthly pleasure.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In Cain stirpe Enoch, qui dedicatio interpretatur, primus nascitur. In electorum vero progenie Enoch septimus fuisse memoratur, quia videlicet reprobi in hac vita, quae ante est, semetipsos aedificando dedicant. Electi vero aedificationis suae dedicationem in fine temporis, id est, in septimo exspectant.
chunks.english: In Cain's lineage, Enoch, whose name means dedication, is born first. But in the line of the elect, Enoch is recorded as the seventh. This is because the reprobate dedicate themselves by building in this present life, which comes before eternity. But the elect await the dedication of their building at the end of time, that is, in the seventh age.
chunks.notes: The number seven represents completion and the end of time in biblical symbolism

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque videmus, plurimos sola cogitare temporalia, honores quaerere, ambiendis rebus inhiare, nihil post hanc vitam quaerere. Quid itaque isti nisi in prima se generatione dedicant?
chunks.english: Very often we see that most people think only of temporal things, seek honors, pant after worldly ambitions, and seek nothing beyond this life. What are such people doing except dedicating themselves in the first generation?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: E contrario videmus electos nihil praesentis gloriae quaerere, libenter inopiam sustinere, mala mundi aequanimiter perpeti, ut possint in fine coronari. Electis ergo Enoch in septima generatione nascitur, quia sui dedicationem gaudii in extremae retributionis gloria requirunt.
chunks.english: On the other hand, we see that the elect seek nothing of present glory, gladly endure poverty, and bear the evils of the world with equanimity so that they may be crowned at the end. So for the elect, Enoch is born in the seventh generation, because they seek the dedication of their joy in the glory of final reward.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omnes iniqui se in primordiis dedicant, quia in hac vita, quae ante est, cordis radicem plantant, ut hic ad votum floreant, et a sequentis patriae deliciis funditus arescant.
chunks.english: All the wicked dedicate themselves at the beginning, because they plant the root of their hearts in this life which comes first, so that they may flourish here according to their wishes but completely wither away from the delights of the homeland that follows.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Amatores hujus saeculi tanto magis exteriora incolunt, quanto interiora sua inculta derelinquunt.
chunks.english: Lovers of this age dwell all the more in external things, the more they leave their interior life uncultivated.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per quemdam Sapientem dicitur: Non apparebis in conspectu Domini vacuus (Exod. XXIII, 15; Moral. VII, c. 29, n. 38). In conspectu Domini vacuus apparet quisquis, praesentem mundum diligens, nihil secum de fructu sui laboris portat.
chunks.english: As a certain wise man says in the book of Exodus, chapter twenty-three, verse fifteen: You shall not appear before the Lord empty-handed. Anyone who loves the present world appears empty before the Lord, because he brings with him nothing from the fruit of his labor.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quicunque terrena et non coelestia diligunt, alius adipiscendis honoribus exsudat, alius multiplicandis facultatibus aestuat, alius promerendis laudibus anhelat. Sed quia cuncta haec hic quisque moriens deserit, ante Dominum vacuus apparet, quia secum ante Judicem nihil tulit.
chunks.english: Whoever loves earthly things and not heavenly things, one person sweats to obtain honors, another burns to multiply his wealth, another pants to earn praises. But because everyone leaves all these things behind when dying, he appears empty before the Lord, because he brought nothing with him before the Judge.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omnis arbor in suo robore juxta terram vasta subsistit, sed crescendo superius angustatur. Et quanto paulisper sublimior, tanto in altum subtilior redditur.
chunks.english: Every tree stands robust and thick near the ground, but as it grows it becomes narrower above. And the higher it rises, the more slender it becomes at the top.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quibus itaque talia arbusta, nisi terrenis mentibus inveniuntur esse similia inferius vasta, superius angusta? Omnes hujus saeculi dilectores in terrenis rebus fortes sunt, in coelestibus debiles; nam pro temporali gloria usque ad mortem desudare appetunt, et pro spe perpetua ne parum quidem in labore subsistunt.
chunks.english: To what are such trees similar except to earthly minds, thick below and narrow above? All lovers of this age are strong in earthly things but weak in heavenly things. For they desire to labor until death for temporal glory, yet for the sake of eternal hope they do not endure even a little labor.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Amatores saeculi pro terrenis lucris quaslibet injurias tolerant; et pro coelesti mercede, vel tenuissimi verbi ferre contumelias recusant. Curis enim saecularibus intenti tanto insensibiliores intus efficiuntur, quanto ad ea quae foris sunt studiosiores intus efficiuntur.
chunks.english: Lovers of this age tolerate any injuries for the sake of earthly profits, yet for heavenly reward they refuse to bear even the slightest verbal insult. For the more intent they are on worldly concerns, the more insensible they become within, and the more zealous they are for external things, the more hardened they become inside.
chunks.notes: Text appears to have a scribal error: 'studiosiores intus' likely should be 'studiosiores exterius' given the context

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omnes qui cogitatione terrena huic saeculo conformantur, per omne quod agunt huic mundo relinquere sui memoriam conantur. Alii bellorum titulis, alii altis aedificiorum moenibus, alii dissertis doctrinarum saecularium libris instanter elaborant, sibique memoriae nomen aedificant.
chunks.english: All who conform themselves to this age through earthly thinking strive in everything they do to leave a memory of themselves to this world. Some labor intensely through the glory of wars, others through towering buildings, others through learned books of secular teaching, and they build for themselves a name in memory.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed cum ipsa ad finem celerius vita percurrat, quid in ea fixum stabit, quando et ipsa celeriter mobilis pertransit? Quantumlibet quisque pro facienda gloria sui nominis elaboret, memoriam suam quasi cinerem posuit, quia hanc citius ventus mortalitatis rapit.
chunks.english: But since life itself runs more swiftly to its end, what will stand fixed in it when life itself passes by so quickly? No matter how much anyone labors to make the glory of his name, he has placed his memory like ashes, because the wind of mortality snatches it away more quickly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et omne quod ex hoc mundo inhianter diligit, protinus amittit. Per Psalmistam dicitur: Qui non accepit in vanum animam suam (Psal. XXIII, 4; Moral. lib. VII, c. 20, n. 38). In vanum namque animam suam accipit, qui ejus vitam negligens, ei curam carnis anteponit; qui sola praesentia cogitans quae se sequantur in perpetuum non attendit.
chunks.english: And he immediately loses everything he greedily loves from this world. As the psalmist says in Psalm twenty-three, verse four: He who has not received his soul in vain. For he receives his soul in vain who, neglecting its life, prefers to it the care of the flesh; who, thinking only of present things, does not attend to what will follow him forever.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT II. De cultu vestimentorum.
chunks.english: Chapter Two: The Vanity of Fine Clothing

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nemo existimet in fluxu atque studio vestium peccatum deesse, quia si hoc culpa non esset, nullo modo Joannem Dominus de vestimenti sui asperitate laudasset.
chunks.english: Let no one think that there is no sin in the pursuit and preoccupation with clothing, because if this were not a fault, the Lord would in no way have praised John for the roughness of his garment.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si cultus vestium culpa non esset, nequaquam Petrus apostolus per Epistolam feminas a pretiosarum vestium appetitu compesceret, dicens: Non in veste pretiosa (I Pet. III, 3).
chunks.english: If the cultivation of clothing were not a fault, the apostle Peter would not have restrained women from the desire for costly garments through his letter, saying in Peter's first letter, chapter three, verse three: Not in costly clothing.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Pensandum nobis summopere est de cultu vestium, quae culpa sit, hoc etiam viros appetere, a quo curavit pastor Ecclesiae et feminas prohibere.
chunks.english: We must consider most carefully regarding the cultivation of clothing what fault it is that even men desire this, from which the shepherd of the Church took care to prohibit even women.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: De Joanne Baptista Veritas ait: Quid existis videre in deserto? hominem mollibus vestitum? ecce qui mollibus vestiuntur in domibus regum sunt (Matth. XI, 8).
chunks.english: The Truth says concerning John the Baptist in the Gospel of Matthew, chapter eleven, verse eight: What did you go out into the desert to see? A man clothed in soft garments? Behold, those who wear soft clothing are in the houses of kings.
chunks.notes: 'Veritas' (Truth) is a standard patristic way of referring to Christ

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Camelorum etenim pilis contextis vestibus Joannes vestitus fuisse describitur. Et quid est dicere: Ecce qui mollibus vestiuntur in domibus Regum sunt, nisi aperta sententia demonstrare quia non coelesti, sed terreno regno militant hi qui pro Deo perpeti aspera refugiunt, sed solis exterioribus dediti praesentis vitae mollitiem et delectationem quaerunt?
chunks.english: For John is described as having been clothed in garments woven from camel's hair. And what does it mean to say: Behold, those who wear soft clothing are in the houses of kings, except to demonstrate with open statement that those fight for an earthly kingdom, not a heavenly one, who refuse to endure harsh things for God but, devoted only to external things, seek the softness and pleasure of present life?

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT III. De his qui terrenis desideriis astringuntur.
chunks.english: Chapter Three: Those Bound by Earthly Desires

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui terrenarum rerum amore vinciuntur, in Deo nullatenus delectantur, quia mundi hujus oblectationibus irretiuntur. Sine delectatione esse anima nunquam potest, nam aut infimis delectatur, aut summis; et quanto acriori cura inardescit ad infima, tanto tempore damnabili frigescit a summis.
chunks.english: Those who are bound by love of earthly things take no delight in God, because they are ensnared by the pleasures of this world. The soul can never exist without delight, for it delights either in the lowest things or in the highest. And the more keenly it burns with concern for the lowest things, the more it grows cold toward the highest things in that condemnable time.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Utraque enim simul et aequaliter amari non possunt. Joannes apostolus sciens spinas amorum saecularium supernae charitatis messem germinare non posse, priusquam aeterni amoris semina proferat, de audientium cordibus sancta verbi manu messem amorum saecularium eradicat, dicens: Nolite diligere mundum, neque ea quae in mundo sunt (I Joan. II, 15).
chunks.english: For both cannot be loved simultaneously and equally. The apostle John, knowing that the thorns of worldly loves cannot allow the harvest of heavenly charity to spring up, first eradicates the harvest of worldly loves from the hearts of his hearers with the holy hand of the word before he spreads the seeds of eternal love, saying in John's first letter, chapter two, verse fifteen: Do not love the world or the things that are in the world.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: De mundi hujus vana dilectione idem apostolus subjungit, dicens: Si quis diligit mundum, non est charitas Patris in eo. Ac si aperte dicat: Utrique se amores in uno corde non capiunt; nec in eo seges supernae charitatis pullulat, in quo illam spinae infimae delectationis necant.
chunks.english: Concerning the vain love of this world, the same apostle adds, saying: If anyone loves the world, the charity of the Father is not in him. As if he were saying openly: Both loves cannot contain themselves in one heart, nor does the crop of heavenly charity sprout in that heart where the thorns of base pleasure kill it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Idem Joannes apostolus ex mundi hujus infima delectatione nascentes punctiones enumerat, dicens: Omne quod est in mundo, concupiscentia carnis et concupiscentia oculorum est, et superbia vitae, quae non est ex Patre, sed de hoc mundo est; et mundus transit, et concupiscentia ejus (Ibid., II, 16).
chunks.english: The same apostle John enumerates the stinging wounds born from the base pleasure of this world, saying: All that is in the world is the lust of the flesh and the lust of the eyes and the pride of life, which is not from the Father but from this world, and the world passes away and its lust.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Delectari in Deo hujus mundi dilectioni substratus homo non valet, quia in ejus mente desideria superna non prodeunt, quam profecto spinae terreni amoris premunt. Cum in hoc saeculo quisque effectum quaesitae felicitatis invenerit, auctorem qui hanc ipsam felicitatem tribuit non requirit, quia privato amore mundum istum assequi non erubescit.
chunks.english: A person laid low by love of this world cannot take delight in God, because heavenly desires do not come forth in his mind, which the thorns of earthly love surely press down. When anyone in this age finds the result of the happiness he sought, he does not seek the Author who granted this very happiness, because he is not ashamed to pursue this world with selfish love.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT IV. De sapientibus hujus saeculi.
chunks.english: Chapter Four: The Wise of This World

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sapientes hujus saeculi admonendi sunt, ut amittant scire quae sciunt, et appetant scire quae nesciunt. Hoc primum destruendum est in illis, quod se sapientes arbitrantur. Scriptum quippe est: Sapientia hujus mundi stultitia est apud Deum (I Cor. III, 19).
chunks.english: The wise of this age must be admonished to lose the knowledge they have and to desire to know what they do not know. This first thing must be destroyed in them: that they think themselves wise. For it is written in Paul's first letter to the Corinthians, chapter three, verse nineteen: The wisdom of this world is foolishness with God.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dicendum est hujus mundi sapientibus ut sapientius stulti fiant, stultam sapientiam deserant, et sapientem Dei stultitiam discant, sicut scriptum est: Si quis videtur inter vos sapiens esse in hoc saeculo, stultus fiat, ut sit sapiens (Ibid., 18).
chunks.english: The wise of this world must be told to become wisely foolish, to abandon foolish wisdom and learn the wise foolishness of God, as it is written: If anyone among you seems to be wise in this age, let him become a fool that he may be wise.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque sapientes hujus saeculi ratiocinationis argumenta melius quam exempla convertunt, ut in suis allegationibus victi jaceant, qui in suis obstinationibus rigidi stabant.
chunks.english: Very often the wise of this age are better converted by arguments of reasoning than by examples, so that they may lie defeated by their own arguments who stood rigid in their own obstinacy.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Magister egregius Paulus apostolus sapientibus et insipientibus debitor, cum Haebraeorum quosdam sapientes, quosdam vero etiam tardiores admoneret, de completione Testamenti Veteris loquens, eorum sapientiam argumento superavit, dicens: Quod enim antiquatur et senescit, prope interitum est (Heb. VIII, 13). Quatenus illos victrix ratio frangeret, et ad meliora argumento loquelae blandae suaderet.
chunks.english: The outstanding teacher, the apostle Paul, debtor to both the wise and foolish, when admonishing some of the Hebrews who were wise and some who were even slower of understanding, speaking about the fulfillment of the Old Testament, overcame their wisdom with an argument, saying in the letter to the Hebrews, chapter eight, verse thirteen: For what is becoming obsolete and growing old is ready to vanish away. So that victorious reasoning might break them and persuade them to better things by the argument of gentle speech.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT V. De his qui judicio Dei obdurantur.
chunks.english: Chapter Five: Those Hardened by God's Judgment

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sicut nemo obsistit largitati Dei vocantis, ita nullus obviat justitiae relinquentis. Non enim cor peccantis Dominus indurat, sed obdurare dicitur, cum ab obduratione non liberat.
chunks.english: Just as no one resists the generosity of God when He calls, so no one opposes the justice of God when He abandons. For the Lord does not harden the heart of the sinner, but He is said to harden when He does not free from hardening.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Obdurare se per justitiam dicit, cum cor reproborum per gratiam non emollit. Recludere Dei est clausis non aperire, sed in suorum operum tenebris peccatores relinquere.
chunks.english: He says He hardens through justice when He does not soften the hearts of the reprobate through grace. For God to shut is not to open what is closed, but to leave sinners in the darkness of their own works.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Misericors Deus tempus nobis relaxat ad poenitentiam, sed cum ejus gratiae patientiam nos ad augmentum vertimus culpae, hoc ipsum tempus, quod ad parcendum pie disposuit, districtius ad feriendum vertit.
chunks.english: Merciful God grants us time for repentance, but when we turn the patience of His grace into an increase of guilt, He turns that very time which He mercifully appointed for sparing into a stricter time for striking.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum reverti quisque ab erroris sui tenebrosis itineribus etiam spatio temporis accepto noluerit, per hoc mala sua ad reatum auget, per quod ea diluere potuit, si converti voluisset.
chunks.english: When anyone, even after receiving time, has refused to return from the dark paths of his error, he increases his guilt for his evils through that very means by which he could have washed them away if he had been willing to turn back.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT VI. De his qui prae amore mundi vel praesentium rerum cupiditate spontaneos tribulationum labores adeunt.
chunks.english: Chapter Six: Those Who Voluntarily Undergo Tribulations for Love of the World

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui prae amore praesentis saeculi a sensu rationis alienus, dum quidquid pro mundo sustinet, leve deputat, laboris amaritudinem, quam tolerat, ignorat, quia nimirum delectabiliter ad cuncta ducitur, in quibus poenaliter fatigatur.
chunks.english: He who, out of love for the present age, is alienated from the sense of reason, while he considers whatever he endures for the world as light, does not perceive the bitterness of the labor he tolerates, because he is led delightfully to all things in which he is painfully exhausted.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Jeremias propheta, humanum genus debriatum voluptatibus suis aspiciens, ait: Inebriavit me absynthio, replevit me amaritudinibus (Thren. III, 16). Ebrius quippe quod patitur nescit. Qui vero absynthio debriatur, et hoc quod sumpsit amarum est, et tamen non intelligit eamdem amaritudinem, qua repletur.
chunks.english: The prophet Jeremiah, seeing the human race drunk on its own pleasures, says in the book of Lamentations, chapter three, verse sixteen: He has made me drunk with wormwood, He has filled me with bitterness. For a drunk man does not know what he suffers. But he who is made drunk with wormwood, what he has taken is bitter, and yet he does not perceive the very bitterness with which he is filled.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sic nimirum sunt amatores hujus saeculi, qui et multimodis laborum tribulationum angustiis amarescunt, et tamen velut ebrii per cupiditatis vesaniam insensibiles redduntur.
chunks.english: Just so are the lovers of this age, who become bitter through manifold hardships and tribulations of labor, and yet like drunkards are rendered insensible through the madness of desire.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Humanum genus recto Dei judicio in voluptatibus suis sibi dimissum, atque per easdem voluptates spontaneis tribulationibus traditum, absynthio est ebrium, quia et amara sunt quae pro hujus saeculi amore tolerat, et tamen eamdem amaritudinem caecitate cupiditatis, quasi insensibilitate ebrietatis, ignorat.
chunks.english: The human race, by the just judgment of God left to its own pleasures and through those same pleasures handed over to voluntary tribulations, is drunk with wormwood. For what it endures for love of this age is bitter, and yet through the blindness of desire, as if through the insensibility of drunkenness, it does not perceive that very bitterness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Mundi quisque gloriam sitiens, dum multas pro ea tribulationes reperit, amarum est quod bibit; sed quia hoc nimis inhianter sumpsit, ejusdem amaritudinis malum discernere prae ipsa ebrietate non sufficit.
chunks.english: Whoever thirsts for the glory of the world, while he finds many tribulations for it, what he drinks is bitter. But because he has taken it too greedily, he cannot discern the evil of that bitterness because of the very drunkenness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Amant perversi homines pro hujus mundi gloria etiam tribulationes; cunctisque pro ea sudoribus libenter serviunt, et gravium laborum jugo devotissime colla submittunt.
chunks.english: Perverse people love even tribulations for the sake of this world's glory. They willingly serve with all labors for it and most devotedly submit their necks to the yoke of heavy burdens.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT VII. De reproborum prosperitate.
chunks.english: Chapter Seven: The Prosperity of the Wicked

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui a Deo avertitur, et prosperatur, tanto perditioni fit proximus, quanto a zelo disciplinae invenitur alienus.
chunks.english: He who turns away from God and prospers becomes all the closer to perdition, the more he is found alienated from the zeal of discipline.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonnulli et iniquitates perpetrant, et gaudere non cessant: Qui nimirum laetantur, cum malefecerint, et exsultant in rebus pessimis (Prov. II, 14). Nonnulli reproborum, cum inique agunt et prosperantur, non sospitate eriguntur, sed insania, qua affligi debuerunt, et inde miseri in exsultatione defluunt, unde a bonis flentur.
chunks.english: Some both perpetrate iniquities and do not cease to rejoice. These indeed rejoice when they have done evil and exult in the worst things, as the book of Proverbs, chapter two, verse fourteen says. Some of the reprobate, when they act wickedly and prosper, are not raised up by health but by insanity with which they ought to be afflicted. And from this, miserable people flow into exultation from which they are wept over by the good.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerique reprobi, dum peccatorum suorum vinculis alligantur, ad justitiae tramitem nequaquam revertuntur. Phreneticorum videlicet sensibus similes, qui insaniam qua praevalent virtutem putant; qui ex morbo esse nesciunt hoc quod amplius sanis possunt, et quasi crevisse viribus aestimant, dum ad vitae terminum per augmenta languoris appropinquant.
chunks.english: Very many of the reprobate, while they are bound by the chains of their sins, in no way return to the path of justice. They are similar to the minds of madmen, who think the insanity by which they excel is virtue, who do not know that what they can do more than healthy people comes from disease, and they think they have grown in strength while they approach the end of life through the increases of sickness.
chunks.notes: Phrenetic refers to a fever causing delirium or madness

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonnunquam reprobi phreneticis similes, quia rationis sensum non habent, flentur et rident. Et tanto in magna exsultatione se dilatant, quanto et insensibiles malum quod patiuntur ignorant.
chunks.english: Sometimes the reprobate, similar to madmen because they do not have the sense of reason, are wept over while they laugh. And they expand themselves in great exultation all the more because, being insensible, they do not know the evil they suffer.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In Ecclesiaste libro scriptum est: Vidi servos in equis, et principes ambulantes quasi servos super terram (Eccle. X, 7). Omnis qui peccat servus est peccati. Servi namque in equis sunt, cum peccatores praesentis vitae dignitatibus efferuntur.
chunks.english: In the book of Ecclesiastes, chapter ten, verse seven, it is written: I saw servants on horses and princes walking like servants on the earth. Everyone who sins is a slave of sin. For servants are on horses when sinners are lifted up by the dignities of present life.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Principes vero quasi servi ambulant, cum multos dignitate virtutum plenos nullus honor erigit. Sed summa hic adversitas velut indignos deorsum premit. Per Psalmistam dicitur: Dormitaverunt qui ascenderunt equos (Psal. LXXV), id est, in morte animae mentis oculos a veritatis luce clauserunt qui in praesentis vitae honore confisi sunt.
chunks.english: But princes walk like servants when many who are full of the dignity of virtues are raised up by no honor, but supreme adversity presses them down as if unworthy. Through the psalmist it is said: Those who mounted horses have slept, that is, in the death of the soul they closed the eyes of the mind from the light of truth who trusted in the honor of present life.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT VIII. De malorum concordia.
chunks.english: Chapter Eight: The Agreement of the Wicked

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dum perversorum nequitia in pace jungitur, profecto eorum malis actibus robur augetur. Quia quo sibi in malitia congruunt, tanto se robustius bonorum afflictionibus illidunt.
chunks.english: When the wickedness of the perverse is joined in peace, strength is certainly increased to their evil deeds. For the more they agree with each other in malice, the more strongly they dash themselves against the afflictions of the good.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ad beatum Job divina voce pro Leviathan dicitur: Membra carnium ejus cohaerentia sibi (Job. XLI, 14). Corpus ejus scuta fusilia, compactum squamis se prementibus una uni conjungitur, et ne spiraculum quidem incedit per eas (Ibid. VI, 7).
chunks.english: To blessed Job the divine voice says concerning Leviathan in the book of Job, chapter forty-one, verse fourteen: The parts of his flesh cling to each other. His body is like shields of cast metal, compacted with scales pressing on each other, one joined to another, and not even a breath passes through them.
chunks.notes: Leviathan is interpreted here as representing Satan

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sequaces quippe Satanae, quo nulla inter se discordiae adversitate divisi sunt, eo in bonorum gravius nece glomerantur. Qui iniquos pace sociat, iniquitati vires administrat, quia bonos deterius deprimunt, quos et unanimiter persequuntur.
chunks.english: For the followers of Satan, since they are divided by no adversity of discord among themselves, mass together all the more heavily for the destruction of the good. He who unites the wicked in peace administers strength to wickedness, because they more severely oppress the good whom they unanimously persecute.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quos similis reatus sociat, concordi pertinacia etiam defensio perversa constipat, ut de facinoribus suis alterna invicem defensione tueantur. Sibi enim quisque metuit, dum admoneri vel corrigi alterum cernit.
chunks.english: Those whom similar guilt unites, perverse defense also binds together with concordant stubbornness, so that they protect each other regarding their crimes with mutual defense. For each one fears for himself when he sees another being admonished or corrected.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et idcirco contra corripientium verba unanimiter assurgit, quia se in altero protegit. Dum vicissim mali superba defensione se protegunt, sanctae exhortationis spiracula ad se nullatenus intrare permittunt.
chunks.english: And therefore they rise up unanimously against the words of those correcting, because each protects himself in the other. While the wicked protect each other by turns with proud defense, they by no means permit the breathing passages of holy exhortation to enter themselves.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Malorum pestiferam concordiam sub Leviathan squamis beatus Job indicat dicens: Una alteri adhaerebunt et tenentes se nequaquam separabuntur (Job. XLI, 8) Qui enim divisi corrigi poterant, in iniquitatum sua rum pertinacia uniti perdurant.
chunks.english: Blessed Job indicates the pestilent agreement of the wicked under the scales of Leviathan, saying: One will cling to another and holding each other they will by no means be separated. For those who could be corrected if divided, united they persist in the stubbornness of their iniquities.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et tanto magis quotidie a cognitione justitiae separabiliores fiunt, quanto a se invicem nulla increpatione separantur. Sicut esse noxium solet, si unitas desit bonis; ita perniciosum est, si non desit malis.
chunks.english: And daily they become more separated from the knowledge of justice, the more they are not separated from each other by any rebuke. Just as it is usually harmful if unity is lacking to the good, so it is destructive if it is not lacking to the evil.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Perversos nonnunquam unitas corroborat, dum concordat; et tanto magis incorrigibiles quanto unanimes facit. De unitate concordantium reproborum per Salomonem dicitur: Stupa collecta Synagoga peccantium (Eccli. XXI, 10). De hac Nahum propheta ait: Sicut spinae se invicem complectuntur, sic convivium eorum pariter potantium (Nahum I, 10).
chunks.english: Sometimes unity strengthens the perverse while it harmonizes, and makes them all the more incorrigible the more unanimous it makes them. Concerning the unity of agreeing reprobates, it is said through Solomon in the book of Sirach, chapter twenty-one, verse ten: The assembly of sinners is like tow gathered together. Concerning this the prophet Nahum says in chapter one, verse ten: As thorns embrace each other, so is their banquet of those drinking together.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Convivium namque reproborum est delectatio temporalium voluptatum; in quo nimirum convivio pariter potant, quia delectationis suae illecebris sese concorditer debriant.
chunks.english: For the banquet of the reprobate is the delight of temporal pleasures. In this banquet indeed they drink together, because they make themselves drunk concordantly with the enticements of their pleasure.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Membra Satanae, id est, iniquos omnes, quos Dei sermo squamarum compactionibus comparat, ad defensionem suam par culpa concordat, sicut ad beatum Job de Leviathan squamis dicitur: Una alteri adhaerebunt, et tenentes se nequaquam separabuntur.
chunks.english: The members of Satan, that is, all the wicked whom the word of God compares to the joining of scales, equal guilt harmonizes for their defense, just as it is said to blessed Job concerning the scales of Leviathan: One will cling to another, and holding each other they will by no means be separated.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tenentes enim se nequaquam separari queunt, quia eo ad defensionem suam vicissim constricti sunt, quo se sibi per omnia similes esse meminerunt.
chunks.english: For holding each other they cannot be separated, because they are mutually bound together for their defense, since they remember that they are in all things similar to each other.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In Exodo scriptum est: Divisa est aqua, et ingressi sunt filii Israel per medium maris sicci (Exodi XIV, 22). Eripiuntur etenim justi, dum per discordiam dividuntur injusti; et electorum vota ad perfectum perveniunt, cum reproborum agmina nexu discordiae confunduntur.
chunks.english: In the book of Exodus, chapter fourteen, verse twenty-two, it is written: The water was divided, and the children of Israel went through the midst of the sea on dry ground. For the just are rescued when the unjust are divided through discord, and the desires of the elect arrive at perfection when the bands of the reprobate are confused by the bond of discord.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum in duas partes unda maris Rubri dividitur, ab electo populo ad terram repromissionis tenditur, quia dum malorum unitas scinditur, bonae mentes ad hoc, quod appetunt, perducuntur.
chunks.english: When the wave of the Red Sea is divided into two parts, the journey to the promised land is made by the chosen people, because while the unity of the wicked is split, good minds are led to what they desire.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si malorum unitas noxia non fuisset, nequaquam divina providentia superbientium linguas in tanta diversitate dissipasset. Si malorum unitas noxia non fuisset, de sanctae Ecclesiae hostibus Propheta non diceret: Praecipita, Domine, et divide linguas eorum (Psal. LIV, 10).
chunks.english: If the unity of the wicked were not harmful, divine providence would never have scattered the tongues of the proud in such diversity. If the unity of the wicked were not harmful, the prophet would not say concerning the enemies of the holy Church in Psalm fifty-four, verse ten: Cast them down, O Lord, and divide their tongues.
chunks.notes: Reference to the Tower of Babel and the confusion of languages

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT IX. De principibus mundi.
chunks.english: Chapter Nine: Princes of the World

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Principes hujus mundi, quo se multis populorum agminibus praelatos esse cognoscunt, eo se sub sanctae fidei disciplina humili mente prosternant, ut non praeesse solummodo studeant, sed multis prodesse gaudeant.
chunks.english: The princes of this world, the more they know themselves to be set over many throngs of peoples, should prostrate themselves with humble mind under the discipline of the holy faith, so that they strive not only to preside but rejoice to benefit many.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam qui subjectarum numerositate plebium attollitur, ipso fastu elationis, quod ad summa conscendere nititur, non immerito casus sui ruina multatur, sicut scriptum est: Dejecisti eos cum allevarentur (Psal. LXXII, 18). Non ait Psalmista, Dejecisti eos postquam elevati sunt; sed Cum allevarentur.
chunks.english: For he who is lifted up by the multitude of subjected peoples is deservedly punished by the ruin of his fall through the very arrogance of pride, because he strives to climb to the heights, as it is written in Psalm seventy-two, verse eighteen: You have cast them down while they were being lifted up. The psalmist does not say, You cast them down after they were lifted up, but while they were being lifted up.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hanc ergo primam ruinam principes timeant, qui privatam gloriam semetipsos diligere non formidant, quia eo plerumque intrinsecus corruunt, quo male extrinsecus surgunt.
chunks.english: Therefore let princes fear this first ruin, who do not fear to love their own private glory, because they usually fall inwardly to the extent that they rise wrongly outwardly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omnes qui in hoc mundo principantur densis cogitationum tumultibus in corde premuntur. Dumque desideriorum turbas intra se excitant, prostratam mentem pede miserae frequentationis calcant.
chunks.english: All who rule in this world are pressed in their hearts by dense tumults of thoughts. And while they stir up crowds of desires within themselves, they trample down the prostrate mind with the foot of wretched busyness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonnunquam princeps eo ipso quo praeeminet caeteris, elatione cogitationis intumescit. Et dum ad usum cuncta subjacent, dum ad votum velociter jussa complentur, dum omnes subditi, si qua bene gesta sunt, laudibus efferunt, male gestis autem nulla auctoritate contradicunt, dum plerumque laudant etiam quod objurgare debuerunt, seductus ab his quae infra subduntur, super se ejus animus extollitur, et dum foras immenso favore circumdatur, intus veritate vacuatur.
chunks.english: Sometimes a prince, by the very fact that he excels others, swells with pride of thought. And while all things are subject to his use, while commands are swiftly fulfilled according to his wishes, while all subjects exalt with praises whatever has been well done but contradict with no authority whatever has been done badly, while they often praise even what they should have rebuked, seduced by these things that are submitted below, his spirit is lifted up above himself. And while he is surrounded outwardly by immense favor, he is emptied inwardly of truth.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Atque oblitus sui in voces se spargit alienas, talemque se credit, qualem se foris audit, non qualem intus discernere debuit.
chunks.english: And forgetting himself, he scatters himself into the voices of others, and he believes himself to be such as he hears himself to be outwardly, not such as he should discern inwardly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque principes subjectos quosque despiciunt, eosque aequales sibi naturae ordine non agnoscunt; et quos sorte potestatis excesserint, transcendisse se etiam vitae meritis credunt.
chunks.english: Often princes despise each of their subjects and do not acknowledge them as equals in the order of nature. And they believe they have surpassed in the merits of life those whom they have exceeded by the lot of power.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cunctis se aestimant amplius sapere, quibus se vident amplius posse. In quodam quippe se constituunt culmine apud semetipsos. Et quia aequa caeteris naturae conditione constringuntur, ex aequo respicere caeteros dedignantur.
chunks.english: They esteem themselves wiser than all those over whom they see themselves to have more power. For they establish themselves in a certain height in their own estimation. And because they are bound by the same condition of nature as others, they disdain to regard others equally.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sicque usque ad ejus similitudinem ducuntur, de quo scriptum est: Omne sublime videt, et ipse est rex super universos filios superbiae (Job XLI, 25). Quisquis potestate honoris praeditus elationis fastu supra subjectos attollitur, ad ejus similitudinem ducitur, qui singulare culmen appetens, et socialem angelorum vitam despiciens, ait: Ascendam super altitudinem nubium, similis ero Altissimo (Isai. XIV, 44).
chunks.english: And so they are led to the likeness of him concerning whom it is written in the book of Job, chapter forty-one, verse twenty-five: He sees everything that is high, and he is king over all the children of pride. Whoever, endowed with the power of honor, is lifted up above his subjects with the arrogance of pride, is led to the likeness of him who, desiring singular height and despising the social life of the angels, said as recorded in the book of Isaiah, chapter fourteen, verse fourteen: I will ascend above the height of the clouds; I will be like the Most High.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Miro ergo judicio intus foveam dejectionis invenit, dum foris se in culmine potestatis extollit. Apostatae quippe angelo similis efficitur, dum homo homini esse similis dedignatur.
chunks.english: Therefore by a wonderful judgment he finds within the pit of abasement while outwardly he lifts himself up in the height of power. For he becomes similar to the apostate angel when a human being disdains to be similar to another human being.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Saul rex humilitatis merito in tumorem superbiae culmine potestatis excrevit; per humilitatem quippe praelatus est, per superbiam reprobatus, Domino attestante, qui ait: Nonne cum esses parvulus in oculis tuis, caput te constitui in tribubus Israel (I Reg. XV, 17)?
chunks.english: King Saul, by the merit of humility, grew into the swelling of pride at the height of power. For through humility he was set over others, but through pride he was rejected, as the Lord testified, saying in the first book of Samuel, chapter fifteen, verse seventeen: When you were little in your own eyes, did I not make you head over the tribes of Israel?
chunks.notes: First Samuel is here labeled with its older name, First Kings

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Parvulum se idem Saul ante potestatem viderat, sed fultus temporali potentia jam se parvulum non videbat. Caeterorum namque comparatione se praeferens magnum se apud se esse judicabat. Miro autem modo, cum apud se parvulus, apud Dominum magnus, cum autem apud se magnus, apud Dominum parvulus fuit.
chunks.english: Saul had seen himself as little before power, but supported by temporal power he no longer saw himself as little. For preferring himself by comparison with others, he judged himself to be great in his own estimation. But in a wonderful way, when he was little in his own eyes, he was great before the Lord; but when he was great in his own eyes, he was little before the Lord.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque dum ex subditorum affluentia principis animus inflatur, in luxum superbiae, ipso potentiae fastigio lenocinante, corrumpitur. Sed aliud est quaelibet bona non esse aliud bona bene habere nescire.
chunks.english: Often, while the spirit of a prince is inflated by the abundance of subjects, it is corrupted into the excess of pride, with the very height of power acting as procurer. But it is one thing not to have any goods, another thing not to know how to possess goods well.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In omne quod princeps agit, tribulatione et angustia vallatur. Quia cor ejus anxietate et suspicione confunditur, sicut per beatum Job dicitur: Terrebit eum tribulatio, et angustia vallabit eum, sicut regem, qui praeparatur ad praelium (Job. XV, 24).
chunks.english: In everything that a prince does, he is surrounded by tribulation and anxiety. For his heart is confused by worry and suspicion, as it is said through blessed Job in chapter fifteen, verse twenty-four: Tribulation will terrify him, and anguish will surround him like a king who is prepared for battle.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quamvis quolibet in loco positus princeps cogitando, loquendo, vel faciendo delinquat, tunc tamen per tria haec animus effrenatius rapitur, cum mundi hujus prosperitate frequentius sublevatur.
chunks.english: Although a prince positioned in any place sins by thinking, speaking, or doing, yet his spirit is carried away more unbridledly through these three things when he is more frequently lifted up by the prosperity of this world.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum praeire se princeps potestate caeteros conspicit, alta de se elate cogitans sentit. Et cum auctoritati vocis a nullo resistitur, lingua licentius per abrupta diffrenatur. Cum plerumque facere principi quod libet licet, juste sibi omne aestimat licere quod libet.
chunks.english: When a prince sees that he surpasses others in power, he thinks lofty things about himself with pride. And when the authority of his voice is resisted by no one, his tongue is unbridled more licentiously into precipices. When a prince is usually allowed to do whatever he wishes, he considers that everything he wishes is justly permitted to him.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nabuchodonosor rex Babyloniae, dum elata mente apud se volveret dicens: Nonne haec est Babylon, quam aedificavi (Dan. IV, 27)? in irrationabile animal protinus versus est. Quod enim factus fuerat perdidit, quia humiliter noluit dissimulare quod fecit.
chunks.english: Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon, while turning over in his mind with pride and saying in the book of Daniel, chapter four, verse twenty-seven: Is this not Babylon which I have built? was immediately turned into an irrational animal. For he lost what he had been made, because he did not wish to humbly conceal what he had done.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et quia elatione cogitationis suae se super homines extulit, ipsum quem communem cum hominibus habuit sensum hominis amisit.
chunks.english: And because he lifted himself above human beings by the pride of his thought, he lost the very sense of humanity that he had in common with other people.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Saepe principes qui in potestate sunt ad subjectorum passim contumelias erumpunt, et hoc quod invigilantes regimini serviunt, per linguae loquacitatem perdunt.
chunks.english: Often princes who are in power break out indiscriminately into insults toward their subjects, and they lose through talkativeness of tongue what they serve by being vigilant in governance.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per Salomon dicitur: Vae tibi, terra, cujus rex est puer, et cujus principes mane comedunt (Eccle. X, 16). Rex quippe puer non incongrue dicitur, dum is qui praeest cura regiminis puerilibus actibus delectatur, et a peccati perpetratione nequaquam subtrahitur.
chunks.english: Through Solomon it is said in the book of Ecclesiastes, chapter ten, verse sixteen: Woe to you, O land, whose king is a boy and whose princes eat in the morning. The king is not inappropriately called a boy when he who presides, despite the care of governance, delights in childish acts and is by no means withdrawn from the perpetration of sin.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde recte Isaias ait: Puer centum annorum morietur, et peccator centum annorum maledictus erit (Isai. LXV, 20), id est, vita quidem pueri in longum trahitur, ut a factis puerilibus corrigatur. Sed si a peccati perpetratione nec temporis longinquitate compescitur, haec ipsa vitae longinquitas, quam per misericordiam accepit, ei ad cumulum maledictionis crescit.
chunks.english: Whence Isaiah rightly says in chapter sixty-five, verse twenty: The boy of a hundred years will die, and the sinner of a hundred years will be accursed. That is, the life of a boy is indeed drawn out long that he may be corrected from childish deeds. But if he is not restrained from the perpetration of sin even by length of time, this very length of life, which he received through mercy, grows for him into a heap of curse.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quis rectum sapiens ignoret quod reges terrae eo a solitudine longe sunt, quo innumeris obsequentium cuneis constipantur? Difficile quippe hi ad requiem tendunt, qui tam duris rationum multiplicium nexibus astringuntur.
chunks.english: What wise person of right understanding does not know that the kings of the earth are far from solitude to the extent that they are crowded by countless wedges of attendants? For those who are bound by such hard bonds of multiple responsibilities proceed with difficulty toward rest.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Scriptura attestante, quae ait: Judicium durissimum in his qui praesunt fiet (Sap. VI, 6). Unde et in Evangelio Veritas dicit: Cui multum datum est, multum quaeretur ab eo (Luc. XII, 48).
chunks.english: As Scripture testifies, which says in the book of Wisdom, chapter six, verse six: The harshest judgment will be made on those who preside. Whence also in the Gospel, the Truth says in the Gospel of Luke, chapter twelve, verse forty-eight: To whom much has been given, much will be required of him.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Beatus Job principatus honore perfunctus, quantae celsitudinis quantaeque fuerit benignitatis, insinuat dicens: Principes cessabant loqui, digitum superponebant ori suo (Job. XXIX, 9). Et iterum: Cumque sederem, quasi rex circumstante exercitu, eram tamen moerentium consolator (Ibid., 25).
chunks.english: Blessed Job, having fulfilled the honor of leadership, suggests how great was his loftiness and how great his kindness, saying in the book of Job, chapter twenty-nine, verse nine: Princes stopped speaking and placed their finger on their mouth. And again in verse twenty-five: And when I sat as king with an army standing around me, I was nevertheless the consoler of those mourning.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Saepe qui potestate principantur ea quae recte faciunt, quia elate cogitant, amittunt; dumque se ad cuncta utiles aestimant, etiam impensae utilitatis meritum sibi damnant.
chunks.english: Often those who rule by power lose the things they do rightly because they think about them with pride. And while they consider themselves useful for everything, they even condemn for themselves the merit of expended usefulness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ut enim quaelibet principis facta digniora sint, necesse est ei apud se semper indigna videantur, ne eadem bona actio agentis cor sublevet, et sublevando plus auctorem de elatione dejiciat, quam ipsos forte quibus impenditur juvet.
chunks.english: For in order that the deeds of a prince be more worthy, it is necessary that they always seem unworthy to him in his own estimation, lest the same good action lift up the heart of the doer, and by lifting him up cast down the author more through pride than it perhaps helps those on whom it is expended.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Potestas plerumque cum habetur, et cogitanda est ad multorum utilitatem, et dissimulanda propter tumorem. Quatenus is qui ea utitur, et ut prodesse debeat, posse se sciat, et ut extolli non debeat, posse se nesciat.
chunks.english: Power, when possessed, should usually both be thought of for the benefit of many and concealed because of swelling. So that he who uses it both knows that he is able so that he should benefit, and does not know that he is able so that he should not be lifted up.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT X. De bonis principibus.
chunks.english: Chapter Ten: Good Princes
chunks.notes: This chapter presents a contrast to the previous one

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Magna est potentia principalis, quia habet apud Deum meritum suum de bona administratione regiminis. Bona est ordine suo potentia principalis, sed cauta regentis indiget vita. Bene hanc exercet, qui scit per illam super culpas erigi, et scit cum illa caeteris aequalitate componi.
chunks.english: Great is princely power, because it has its merit before God from good administration of governance. Princely power is good in its order, but it needs the cautious life of the one governing. He exercises it well who knows to be raised up through it over faults, and knows to be composed with others in equality through it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Humana mens plerumque extollitur, etiam cum nulla potestate fulcitur; quanto magis in altum se erigit, cum se ei etiam potestas adjungit? Et tamen corrigendis aliorum vitiis apta exsecutione praeparatur. Unde et per Paulum dicitur: Minister enim Dei est vindex in iram (Rom. XIII, 4).
chunks.english: The human mind is usually lifted up even when it is supported by no power. How much more does it raise itself on high when power is also joined to it? And yet it is prepared with suitable execution for correcting the vices of others. Whence it is also said through Paul in his letter to the Romans, chapter thirteen, verse four: For he is God's minister, an avenger for wrath.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum potentiae principalis culmen suscipitur, summa cura vigilandum est, ut sciat quisque et sumere ex illa quod adjuvat, et expugnare quod tentat.
chunks.english: When the height of princely power is undertaken, it must be watched with supreme care so that anyone knows both to take from it what helps and to fight against what tempts.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum mundi hujus potestate principes fulciuntur, tanto sub mojori mentis disciplina se redigunt, quantum sibi per impatientiam potestatis suaderi illicita quasi licentius sciunt. Cor namque a consideranda gloria reprimunt, linguam ab immoderata locutione restringunt, opus ab inquietudinis vagatione custodiunt.
chunks.english: When princes are supported by the power of this world, they reduce themselves under greater discipline of mind to the degree that they know that unlawful things are persuaded to them more licentiously through the impatience of power. For they restrain the heart from considering glory, restrict the tongue from immoderate speech, and guard their work from the wandering of restlessness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sciendum summopere est, quia rex qui praeparatur ad praelium sic de hoste suspectus est, ut eumdem quoque quem ducit exercitum metuat, ne labefactetur et destitutione militum jaculis pateat inimicorum.
chunks.english: It must be known most especially that a king who is prepared for battle is so suspicious of the enemy that he also fears the very army he leads, lest it be weakened and he be exposed to the javelins of enemies by the desertion of soldiers.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque rex qui praeparatur ad praelium nimia vallatur angustia, quia videlicet formidat ne subito suos amittat milites, et per aliquam desidiam incursantium inimicorum insidiis occupetur.
chunks.english: Often a king who is prepared for battle is surrounded by excessive anxiety, because he fears lest he suddenly lose his soldiers and be seized by the snares of attacking enemies through some neglect.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Princeps terrenam rempublicam gerens aliter punit civem interius delinquentem, atque aliter hostem exterius rebellantem. In illo jura sua consulit, eumque sub verbis dignae invectionis addicit. Contra hostem vero bellum movet, instrumenta perditionis exercet, dignaque ejus malitiae tormenta retribuit: de malo vero ejus quid lex habeat non requirit; neque enim lege necesse est perimi, qui lege nunquam potuit teneri.
chunks.english: A prince managing an earthly republic punishes differently a citizen sinning within and an enemy rebelling without. In the former case he consults his laws and condemns him under words of worthy rebuke. But against an enemy he wages war, exercises instruments of destruction, and repays torments worthy of his malice. But he does not inquire what the law has concerning his evil, for it is not necessary to be destroyed by law for one who could never be held by law.
chunks.notes: This analogy is being used to explain God's different treatment of those within and outside the covenant

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The Location and Nature of Hell
chunks.latin: CAPUT XX. De inferno superiore atque inferiore.
chunks.english: Chapter 20: The Location and Nature of Hell

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonnulli in quadam terrarum parte infernum esse putaverunt; alii vero hunc sub terra esse aestimant (Dialog. IV, 42). Sed tamen hoc animum pulsat, quia si idcirco infernum dicimus, quia inferius jacet, quod terra ad coelum est, hoc esse infernus debet ad terram. Unde fortasse dicitur: Liberasti animam meam ex inferno inferiori (Psal. LXXXV, 13), ut infernus superior terra, infernus vero inferior sub terra esse videatur.
chunks.english: Some have thought that hell exists in a certain part of the earth, while others believe it is beneath the earth. But this question troubles the mind: if we call it hell because it lies below the earth as the earth lies below heaven, then hell must be to the earth what the earth is to heaven. This may be why Scripture says, as in the Psalms, chapter eighty-five, verse thirteen, 'You have delivered my soul from the lower hell'—suggesting that there is an upper hell, which would be the earth, and a lower hell beneath the earth.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Joannis vox in ea aestimatione concordat, qui cum signatum librum septem sigillis vidisse se diceret, quia nemo inventus est dignus, neque in coelo, neque in terra, neque subtus terram aperire librum, et solvere signacula ejus, adjunxit: Et ego flebam multum (Apoc. V, 4). Quem tamen postmodum librum per leonem de tribu Juda dicit aperiri.
chunks.english: The voice of John agrees with this estimation. When he said he saw a book sealed with seven seals, he added—as recorded in the book of Revelation, chapter five, verse four—'And I wept much, because no one was found worthy, neither in heaven, nor on earth, nor under the earth, to open the book and loose its seals.' Yet he says that afterward the book was opened by the Lion of the tribe of Judah.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In quo videlicet libro, quid aliud quam sacra Scriptura signatur? Quam solus Redemptor noster aperuit, qui homo factus moriendo, resurgendo, ascendendo, cuncta mysteria quae in ea fuerant clausa patefecit. Nullus in coelo dignus inventus est aperire librum, quia neque angelus; nullus in terra, quia neque homo vivens in corpore; nullus subtus terram, quia neque animae corpore exutae aperire nobis, praeter Dominum, sacri eloquii secreta potuerunt.
chunks.english: What else is signified in that book but Sacred Scripture? Only our Redeemer opened it—he who became human and, by dying, rising, and ascending, revealed all the mysteries that had been sealed within it. No one in heaven was found worthy to open the book, not even an angel; no one on earth, not even a man living in the body; no one under the earth, because not even souls stripped of their bodies could open for us the secrets of sacred Scripture, except the Lord.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum ad solvendum librum nullus sub terra inventus dignus dicitur, quid obstet non video ut sub terra esse infernus credatur.
chunks.english: Since no one under the earth was found worthy to open the book, I see no obstacle to believing that hell is under the earth.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXI. De igne purgatorio, quod post mortem peccata laxari credantur.
chunks.english: Chapter 21: Purgatorial Fire and Post-Mortem Purification

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omne pondus fabricae fundamentum portat, quia mores simul omnium solus Redemptor noster tolerat (Lib. II, in Ezech., homil. 1, n. 5). Scriptum quippe est: Fundamentum enim aliud nemo potest ponere praeter id quod positum est, qui est Christus Jesus (I Cor. III, 11). Portat fundamentum lapides, et a lapidibus non portatur; quia Redemptor noster omnia nostra tolerat, sed in ipso malum non fuit quod tolerari debuisset.
chunks.english: The foundation bears the whole weight of the building, because our Redeemer alone bears the conduct of all people. For it is written, in Paul's first letter to the Corinthians, chapter three, verse eleven: 'No one can lay a foundation other than that which has been laid, which is Jesus Christ.' The foundation bears the stones, and is not borne by the stones, because our Redeemer bears all that is ours, but in him there was no evil that needed to be borne.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ipse quippe fundamentum fundamentorum est, quia et origo est inchoantium, et constantia robustorum (Moral. XXVIII, c. 5, n. 14; Dial. IV, 39). Egregius praedicator ait: Si quis autem superaedificat supra fundamentum hoc aurum, argentum, lapides pretiosos, ligna, fenum, stipulam, uniuscujusque opus manifestum erit. Dies enim Domini declarabit, quia in igne revelabitur; et uniuscujusque opus, quale sit, ignis probabit. Si cujus opus manserit, quod superaedificavit, mercedem accipiet. Si cujus opus arserit, detrimentum patietur; ipse autem salvus erit, sic tamen quasi per ignem (I Cor. III, 12).
chunks.english: He is the foundation of foundations, because he is the source for beginners and the strength of those who are strong. The excellent preacher says, in Paul's first letter to the Corinthians, chapter three, verse twelve: 'If anyone builds on this foundation with gold, silver, precious stones, wood, hay, or straw, each one's work will become manifest. For the Day of the Lord will declare it, because it will be revealed by fire; and the fire will test each one's work, of what sort it is. If anyone's work which he has built endures, he will receive a reward. If anyone's work is burned, he will suffer loss; but he himself will be saved, yet so as through fire.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quamvis hoc, quod superius protulimus de igne tribulationis in hac nobis vita adhibito possit intelligi, tamen si quis hoc de igne futurae purgationis accipiat, pensandum sollicite est quia illum dixit per ignem posse salvari, non qui super hoc fundamentum ferrum, aes, vel plumbum aedificat, id est, peccata majora, et idcirco duriora, atque tunc jam insolubilia; sed ligna, fenum, stipulam, id est, peccata minima, atque levissima, quae ignis facile consumat.
chunks.english: Although what we have said above can be understood as referring to the fire of tribulation applied to us in this life, if anyone takes it to mean the fire of future purgation, we must carefully consider that he said the person can be saved through fire—not one who builds on this foundation iron, brass, or lead, that is, greater sins that are therefore harder and already insoluble, but wood, hay, and straw, that is, the smallest and lightest sins, which fire easily consumes.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hoc tamen sciendum est, quia illic saltem de minimis nil quisque purgationis obtinebit, nisi bonis hoc actibus in hac adhuc vita positus, ut illic obtineat, promereatur.
chunks.english: Yet it must be known that no one will obtain any purification there, even for the smallest sins, unless while still placed in this life he merits it through good deeds, so that he may obtain it there.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXII. De ira vel indignatione Dei.
chunks.english: Chapter 22: God's Wrath and Unchanging Justice

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ira omnipotentis Dei in hoc quotidie vim suae districtionis peragit, quod viventes indigne dignis suppliciis demergit (Moral. XII, c. 10, n. 14). Quae ira nunc equidem transit, sed in fine pertransit, quia modo agitur, sed in mundi termino consummatur. Iste furor quantum ad electorum animas, in Redemptoris nostri adventum pertransiit, quia eas ab inferni claustris ad paradisi gaudia mediator Dei et hominum, dum ipse illuc pie descenderet, reduxit.
chunks.english: The wrath of almighty God daily carries out the force of his strict judgment in that he plunges those living unworthily into deserved punishments. This wrath is passing even now, but it will fully pass at the end, because it is now at work, but will be consummated at the world's end. This fury, as far as the souls of the elect are concerned, passed away at the coming of our Redeemer, because he—the mediator between God and humanity—brought them back from the prisons of hell to the joys of paradise when he descended there in mercy.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sciendum est quod furoris nomen Divinitati non congruit, quia naturam Dei simplicem perturbatio nulla confundit; unde ei dicitur: Tu autem dominator virtutis cum tranquillitate judicas, et cum magna reverentia disponis nos (Sap. XII, 18; Moral. XXXII, c. 7, n. 9). Creator omnium eo summe immortalis est, quo creaturae more mutabilis non est. Scriptum quippe est: Tu autem, Domine, cum tranquillitate judicas (Ibid.).
chunks.english: We must know that the name of fury does not properly belong to the Divinity, because no disturbance confounds the simple nature of God. As the book of Wisdom, chapter twelve, verse eighteen says: 'You, Lord of power, judge with tranquility, and with great reverence you dispose of us.' The Creator of all is supremely immortal in that he is not changeable in the manner of creatures. For it is written: 'You, Lord, judge with tranquility.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et iterum: Deserta facta est terra a facie irae columbae, a facie furoris Domini (Jer. XXV, 38). Quod propheta iram columbae praedixerat, hoc furorem Domini subjunxit. Columba namque valde simplex est animal, et quia in Deum nulla furoris inaequalitas serpit, furorem Domini iram columbae nominavit. Ut vim divinae districtionis propheta imperturbabilem demonstraret, et iram dixit, et columbae; ac si apertius diceret: districtum judicium inconcussus exerit, qui permanens mansuetus punit.
chunks.english: And again, from the prophet Jeremiah, chapter twenty-five, verse thirty-eight: 'The land has been made desolate before the wrath of the dove, before the fury of the Lord.' What the prophet foretold as the wrath of the dove, he then called the fury of the Lord. For the dove is a very simple creature, and because no inequality of fury creeps into God, he called the fury of the Lord the wrath of the dove. So that the prophet might demonstrate the undisturbed force of divine judgment, he spoke of both wrath and the dove—as if saying more openly: he who remains gentle exercises strict judgment, and punishes without disturbance.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXIII. De flagellis divinis electis, vel reprobis illatis.
chunks.english: Chapter 23: Divine Chastisements: Purposes and Types

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Percussionum diversa sunt genera (Praef. in B. Job, c. 5, n. 12). Alia namque est percussio, qua peccator percutitur, ut sine retractatione puniatur; alia, qua peccator percutitur, ut corrigatur; alia, qua nonnunquam quisque percutitur, non ut praeterita corrigat, sed ne ventura committat; alia, qua plerumque percutitur, per quam nec praeterita culpa corrigitur, nec futura prohibetur; sed ut dum inopinata salus percussionem sequitur, salvantis virtus cognita ardentius ametur; cumque innoxius flagello atteritur, ei per patientiam meritorum summa cumuletur.
chunks.english: There are different kinds of blows. One kind of blow is when a sinner is struck to be punished without possibility of retraction. Another is when a sinner is struck to be corrected. Another is when someone is sometimes struck, not to correct past sins, but to prevent future ones. Another is when someone is often struck, through which neither past guilt is corrected nor future sin prevented, but rather, when unexpected deliverance follows the blow, the power of the Savior becomes known and is loved more ardently; and when an innocent person is worn down by the scourge, the highest sum of merits is heaped up for him through patience.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Aliquando peccator percutitur, ut absque retractatione puniatur, sicut periturae Judaeae dicitur: Plaga inimici percussi te castigatione crudeli (Jer. XXX, 14). Et rursum: Quid clamas ad me super contritione tua? Insanabilis est dolor tuus (Ibid., 15).
chunks.english: Sometimes a sinner is struck to be punished without retraction, as is said to doomed Judea in the prophet Jeremiah, chapter thirty, verse fourteen: 'I have struck you with the blow of an enemy, with cruel chastisement.' And again, verse fifteen: 'Why do you cry out to me over your crushing? Your pain is incurable.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Aliquando peccator percutitur, ut corrigatur, sicut cuidam in Evangelio dicitur: Ecce sanus factus es, jam noli peccare, ne tibi deterius aliquid contingat (Joan. V, 14). Verba enim Salvatoris indicant quia peccata praecedentia habiti vim doloris exigebant.
chunks.english: Sometimes a sinner is struck to be corrected, as is said to someone in the Gospel of John, chapter five, verse fourteen: 'See, you have been made well; sin no more, lest something worse happen to you.' For the Savior's words indicate that previous sins demanded the force of suffering experienced.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Aliquando quisque non pro praeterita culpa diluenda, sed pro futura vitanda percutitur, quod aperte Paulus de semetipso testatur, dicens: Ne magnitudo revelationum extollat me, datus est mihi stimulus carnis meae, angelus Satanae, qui me colaphizet (II Cor. XII, 7). Qui enim non ait Quia extulit, sed Ne extollat, aperte indicat quod percussione illa, ne eveniat, compescitur, non autem, quae evenit, culpa purgatur.
chunks.english: Sometimes someone is struck not to wash away past guilt, but to avoid future sin, as Paul openly testifies about himself in his second letter to the Corinthians, chapter twelve, verse seven: 'Lest the greatness of the revelations should exalt me, a thorn in my flesh was given to me, an angel of Satan to buffet me.' For since he does not say 'because it exalted' but 'lest it exalt,' he openly indicates that by that blow he is restrained lest it happen, not that guilt which has happened is purged.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonnunquam quisque nec pro praeterita nec pro futura iniquitate percutitur, sed ut sola divinae virtutis potentia ex amputata percussione monstretur. Unde cum Domino in Evangelio de caeco nato diceretur: Hic peccavit, aut parentes ejus, ut caecus nasceretur (Joan. IX, 2)? Respondit Dominus, dicens: Neque hic peccavit, neque parentes ejus, sed ut manifestentur opera Dei (Ibid., 3). In qua manifestatione quid agitur, nisi ut ex flagello meritorum virtus augeatur, et cum nulla praeterita iniquitas tergitur, magna de patientia fortitudo generetur (Moral. XII, 38, n. 43)?
chunks.english: Sometimes someone is struck neither for past nor future iniquity, but so that the power of divine virtue alone might be shown through the removal of the blow. Thus when it was said to the Lord in the Gospel of John, chapter nine, verse two, about the man born blind: 'Did this man sin, or his parents, that he was born blind?' The Lord answered, saying in verse three: 'Neither this man sinned, nor his parents, but that the works of God might be manifested.' In this manifestation, what is accomplished except that virtue of merits is increased by the scourge, and when no past iniquity is wiped away, great strength is generated from patience?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omnipotens Deus prave agentibus, non solum ventura supplicia reservat, sed eorum corda hic etiam ubi delinquunt, poenis implicat, ut eo ipso quo peccant, semetipsos feriant, ut semper trepidi, semperque suspecti mala ab aliis pati metuant, quae se aliis fecisse meminerunt. Qui ita percutitur, ut vires illius a percussione superentur, non hunc Dominus jam quasi filium per disciplinam, sed quasi hostem per iram ferit (Moral. XIV, 37, n. 45). Cum virtutem nostrae patientiae flagella transeunt, valde metuendum est ne, peccatis nostris exigentibus, non quasi filii a patre, sed quasi hostes a Domino feriamur.
chunks.english: Almighty God reserves not only future punishments for those acting wickedly, but also entangles their hearts here, even where they sin, with penalties, so that by the very act of sinning they strike themselves, always trembling, always suspicious, fearing to suffer from others the evils they remember having done to others. When someone is so struck that his strength is overcome by the blow, the Lord no longer strikes him as a son through discipline, but as an enemy through wrath. When blows exceed the strength of our patience, we must greatly fear lest, our sins demanding it, we be struck not as sons by a father, but as enemies by the Lord.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The World's End and Its Sufferings
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXIV. De variis percussionibus mundi.
chunks.english: Chapter 24: The World's End and Its Sufferings

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui Deum diligunt, ex mundi fine vel percussione gaudere atque hilarescere jubentur, quia videlicet eum quem amant mox inveniunt, dum transit is quem non amaverunt (Lib. I in Ev., homil. 1, n. 3). Absit ne fidelis quisque Deum videre non desideret, et de mundi percussionibus lugeat, quem finiri eis ipsis percussionibus non ignorat.
chunks.english: Those who love God are commanded to rejoice and be glad at the world's end or its blows, because they will soon find the one they love, while that which they did not love passes away. God forbid that any faithful person should not desire to see God, and should mourn the world's blows, knowing that the world is being ended by those very blows.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Scriptum est: Quicunque voluerit amicus esse saeculi hujus, inimicus Dei constituitur (Jacob. IV, 4). Qui ergo appropinquante mundi fine non gaudet, amicus illius esse testatur, ac per hoc inimicus Dei esse convincitur. Absit hoc a fidelium cordibus, ut de mundi fine non hilarescant. Absit ab his qui et esse aliam vitam per fidem credunt, et eam per operationem diligunt.
chunks.english: It is written in the letter of James, chapter four, verse four: 'Whoever desires to be a friend of this world is constituted an enemy of God.' Therefore, whoever does not rejoice as the world's end approaches testifies that he is its friend, and is thereby proven to be an enemy of God. God forbid that it should be far from the hearts of the faithful that they not rejoice at the world's end. God forbid that this be true of those who believe through faith that another life exists, and love it through their actions.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ex mundi destructione lugere, eorum est qui radices cordis in ejus amore plantaverunt, qui sequentem vitam non quaerunt, qui illam neque esse suspicantur. Nos, qui illa coelestis patriae gaudia aeterna cognovimus, festinare ad ea quantocius debemus. Optandum nobis est citius pergere, atque ad illam vitam felicitate perpetua pervenire.
chunks.english: To mourn the world's destruction belongs to those who have planted the roots of their hearts in love of it, who do not seek the life to come, who do not even suspect that it exists. We, who have come to know the eternal joys of the heavenly homeland, ought to hasten toward them as quickly as possible. We should desire to go more swiftly and to reach that life of perpetual happiness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quibus malis non mundus urgetur? Quae nos tristitia, quae adversitas non angustat? Quid est vita mortalis, nisi via? Et quale sit primo opere perpendamus, in labore viae lassescere, et tamen eamdem viam nolle finiri. Quod autem calcari mundus ac despici debeat, Redemptor noster provida comparatione manifestat, cum in Evangelio dicit: Videte ficulneam et omnes arbores, cum producunt jam ex se fructum, scitis quoniam prope est aestas. Ita et vos cum videritis haec fieri, scitote quoniam prope est regnum Dei. Ac si aperte dicens: Quia sicut ex fructu arborum vicina aestas agnoscitur; ita ex ruina mundi prope esse cognoscitur regnum Dei.
chunks.english: By what evils is the world not pressed? What sorrow, what adversity does not afflict us? What is mortal life but a journey? And what a thing it is—consider it first—to grow weary in the labor of the journey, yet not wish for that same journey to end. But that the world should be trampled and despised, our Redeemer makes clear by a wise comparison when he says in the Gospel: 'Look at the fig tree and all the trees; when they now put forth fruit, you know that summer is near. So also, when you see these things happening, know that the kingdom of God is near.' As if saying openly: Just as the approach of summer is recognized from the fruit of trees, so from the ruin of the world the kingdom of God is known to be near.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The Conversion of the Jews at the End
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXV. De Judaici populi circa finem mundi conversionem.
chunks.english: Chapter 25: The Conversion of the Jews at the End

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sancta Ecclesia in primitiis suis multitudine gentium fecundata, vix in mundi fine Judaeos quos invenerit suscipiet (In Praef. l. IV, Mor. c. 4). Extrema colligens, eos quasi reliquias frugum ponet. De quibus Judaici populi reliquiis Esaias dicit: Si fuerit numerus filiorum Israel quasi arena maris, reliquiae salvae fient (Isai. X, 22).
