chunks.english: The holy Church, made fruitful in its beginnings by the multitude of nations, will scarcely at the world's end receive the Jews whom it finds. Gathering the last remnants, it will place them like the remnants of crops. Concerning these remnants of the Jewish people, the prophet Isaiah, chapter ten, verse twenty-two says: 'If the number of the children of Israel be as the sand of the sea, a remnant shall be saved.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Elia veniente, promittitur quod reducat corda filiorum ad patres eorum ut doctrina quae nunc a Judaeorum corde ablata est, tunc, miserante Domino, redeat, quando hoc de Domino intelligere coeperint filii, quod praedicaverunt patres (Malach. IV, 6; Moral. XI, c. 15, n. 24). Esau, qui tarde ad patrem redit, significat quod Judaicus populus ad placendum Deo sero revertitur (Lib. I in Ezech., homil. 6, n. 6). Cui hoc in benedictione dicitur: Tempusque erit, cum solvatur jugum de collo tuo (Gen. XXVII, 40).
chunks.english: With Elijah's coming, it is promised that he will turn the hearts of the children back to their fathers—as the prophet Malachi, chapter four, verse six says—so that the teaching which has now been taken from the heart of the Jews will then return, the Lord having mercy, when the children begin to understand concerning the Lord what the fathers preached. Esau, who returns late to his father, signifies that the Jewish people returns late to pleasing God. To him this is said in the blessing in the book of Genesis, chapter twenty-seven, verse forty: 'And the time will come when you will shake off his yoke from your neck.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Judaicus populus in fine mundi liberabitur a servitute peccati, sicut scriptum est: Donec plenitudo gentium intraret, et sic omnis Israel salvus fieret (Rom. XI, 25). Et sicut Propheta ait: Si fuerit numerus filiorum Israel sicut arena maris, reliquiae salvae fient (Isai. X, 22). Sancta Ecclesia adhuc in hac vita posita pro laboribus quos sustinet duplicia munera recipit (Praef. in B. Job, c. X, n. 20); cum, susceptis, ut diximus, plene gentibus, in fine mundi Judaeorum etiam ad se corda convertit, sicut scriptum est: Donec plenitudo gentium intraret, et sic omnis Israel salvus fieret (Rom. XI, 25).
chunks.english: The Jewish people will be freed from the servitude of sin at the world's end, as it is written in Paul's letter to the Romans, chapter eleven, verse twenty-five: 'Until the fullness of the Gentiles has come in, and so all Israel will be saved.' And as the Prophet says in Isaiah, chapter ten, verse twenty-two: 'If the number of the children of Israel be as the sand of the sea, a remnant shall be saved.' The holy Church, still placed in this life, receives double gifts for the labors it sustains, since, having received the Gentiles fully as we said, at the world's end it also converts the hearts of the Jews to itself, as it is written: 'Until the fullness of the Gentiles has come in, and so all Israel will be saved.'

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXVI. De Antichristi temporibus.
chunks.english: Chapter 26: The Times of Antichrist

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Antiquus hostis extrema mundi atrocius tentaturus aggreditur, quia tanto fit ferventior ad saevitiam, quanto se viciniorem sentit ad poenam (Moral., lib. XXXIV, c. 1, n. 1). Considerat quippe quod juxta sit ut licentiam nequissimae libertatis amittat; et quantum brevitate temporis angustatur, tantum multiplicitate crudelitatis expanditur. In Apocalypsi dicitur: Vae terrae, et mari, quia descendit diabolus ad vos habens iram magnam, sciens quod modicum tempus habet (Apoc. XII, 12).
chunks.english: The ancient enemy approaches to tempt more terribly at the world's end, because he becomes all the more fervent toward cruelty the closer he feels himself to punishment. For he considers that it is near that he should lose the license of his most wicked freedom; and the more he is constrained by the brevity of time, the more he expands in the multiplication of cruelty. In the book of Revelation, chapter twelve, verse twelve, it says: 'Woe to the earth and the sea, because the devil has come down to you having great wrath, knowing that he has little time.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tunc ergo in magnae irae fervorem se dilatat, ne qui in beatitudine stare non potuit in damnationis suae foveam cum paucis ruat. Quidquid diabolus nequiter valuerit, in fine mundi callidius exquirit; tunc cervicem superbiae altius erigit, et per damnatum illum quem gestat hominem omne quod temporaliter praevalet nequiter ostendit.
chunks.english: Then he expands himself in the fervor of great wrath, lest he who could not stand in blessedness should fall into the pit of his own damnation with only a few. Whatever the devil has wickedly been able to do, he pursues more cunningly at the world's end. Then he raises the neck of pride higher, and through that damned man whom he carries, he wickedly displays all that temporarily prevails.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Idem Joannes ait: Cauda draconis trahebat tertiam partem stellarum coeli, et misit eas in terram (Ibid., XII, 4). Coelum namque est Ecclesia, quae in hac nocte vitae praesentis, dum in se innumeras sanctorum virtutes continet, radiantibus desuper sideribus fulget (Moral. lib. XXXII, c. 15, n. 25). Draconis cauda in terram stellas dejicit, quia illa Satanae extremitas, per audaciam assumpti hominis erecta, quosdam, quos velut electos Dei in Ecclesia invenit, obtinendo reprobos ostendit.
chunks.english: The same John says in Revelation, chapter twelve, verse four: 'The dragon's tail drew a third part of the stars of heaven, and cast them to the earth.' For heaven is the Church, which in this night of the present life, while it contains in itself the countless virtues of the saints, shines with stars radiating from above. The dragon's tail casts stars to the earth, because that extremity of Satan, raised up through the audacity of the man he has assumed, shows certain ones whom he finds in the Church as if they were God's elect to be reprobate by prevailing over them.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Stellas de coelo in terram cadere, est relicta nonnullos spe coelestium, illo duce ad ambitum gloriae saecularis inhiare. Iste igitur draco misit caudam, et extraxit tertiam partem stellarum (Moral., lib. IV, c. 10, n. 17). Draconis enim cauda stellarum pars trahitur, quia extrema persuasione Antichristi quidam qui videntur lucere rapientur.
chunks.english: For stars to fall from heaven to earth means that some, abandoning the hope of heavenly things, under that leader's guidance gape after the pursuit of worldly glory. Therefore this dragon cast forth his tail and drew out a third part of the stars. For a part of the stars is drawn by the dragon's tail, because by the final persuasion of Antichrist, certain ones who seem to shine will be snatched away.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonnulli de Tribu Dan venire Antichristum ferunt, sicut per Jacob patriarcham dicitur: Fiat Dan coluber in via, cerastes in semita, et mordens ungulas equi, ut cadat ascensor ejus retro (Gen. XLIX, 17; Moral., lib. XXXI, c. 24, n. 43). Hoc in loco Dan et coluber asseritur, et mordens. Unde non immerito dum Israeliticus populus terras in castrorum partitione susciperet, primus Dan ad aquilonem castrametatus est: illum scilicet signans qui in corde suo dixerat: Sedebo in monte testamenti, in lateribus Aquilonis; ascendam super altitudinem nubium, similis ero Altissimo (Isai. XIV, 13).
chunks.english: Some report that Antichrist will come from the tribe of Dan, as is said through the patriarch Jacob in the book of Genesis, chapter forty-nine, verse seventeen: 'Let Dan be a serpent in the way, a horned viper in the path, biting the horse's heels so that its rider falls backward.' In this place Dan is declared both serpent and biting. Hence not undeservedly, when the Israelite people received lands in the division of camps, Dan first camped toward the north—signifying him who said in his heart, as Isaiah chapter fourteen, verse thirteen records: 'I will sit on the mount of assembly, in the far reaches of the north; I will ascend above the heights of the clouds; I will make myself like the Most High.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: De quo et per prophetam dicitur: A Dan auditus est fremitus equorum ejus (Jerem. VIII, 16). Antichristus scilicet non solum coluber, sed etiam cerastes vocatur. Kerata enim Graece cornua dicitur; serpensque hic cornutus esse perhibetur, per quem digne ejusdem Antichristi adventus asseritur, quia contra vitam fidelium cum morsu pestiferae praedicationis armatur etiam cornibus potestatis.
chunks.english: And concerning him the prophet Jeremiah, chapter eight, verse sixteen says: 'From Dan was heard the snorting of his horses.' Antichrist is called not only serpent but also horned viper. For in Greek, kerata means horns, and this serpent is said to be horned, through which the coming of that same Antichrist is fittingly asserted, because against the life of the faithful he is armed not only with the bite of pestilent preaching but also with the horns of power.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quis nesciat semitam angustiorem esse quam viam? Fit ergo Dan coluber in via, quia in praesentis vitae latitudine eos ambulare provocat, quibus quasi parcendo blanditur; sed in via mordet, quia eos, quibus libertatem tribuit, erroris sui veneno consumit. Fit cerastes in semita, quia quos fideles reperit, et sese ad praecepti coelestis angusta itinera constringentes, non solum nequitia callidae persuasionis impetit, sed etiam terrore potestatis premit; et in persecutionis languore, post beneficia fictae dulcedinis, exercet cornua potestatis.
chunks.english: Who does not know that a path is narrower than a way? Therefore Dan becomes a serpent in the way, because in the breadth of the present life he provokes them to walk whom he flatters as if sparing them; but in the way he bites, because those to whom he grants liberty, he consumes with the venom of his error. He becomes a horned viper in the path, because those whom he finds faithful and constraining themselves to the narrow roads of heavenly commandment, he attacks not only with the wickedness of cunning persuasion but also oppresses with the terror of power; and in the languor of persecution, after the benefits of feigned sweetness, he exercises the horns of power.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Equus iste, cujus ungulas cerastes mordere dicitur, hunc mundum insinuat, qui per elationem suam incursu labentium temporum spumat. Et quia Antichristus extrema mundi apprehendere nititur, cerastes iste equi ungulas mordere perhibetur. Ungulas quippe equi mordere, est extrema saeculi feriendo contingere: Ut cadat ascensor ejus retro (Gen. XLIX, 17). Plebs infidelis Judaea erroris sui laqueis capta, pro Christo Antichristum exspectat.
chunks.english: This horse, whose hooves the horned viper is said to bite, suggests this world, which through its exaltation foams with the rushing of passing times. And because Antichrist strives to seize the world's extremities, this horned viper is said to bite the horse's hooves. To bite the horse's hooves is to strike and reach the world's end, 'so that its rider falls backward.' The faithless Jewish people, caught in the snares of their error, await Antichrist instead of Christ.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Bene Jacob eodem loco repente in electorum voce conversus est, dicens: Salutare tuum exspectabo, Domine (Ibid. 18), id est, non sicut infideles Antichristum, sed eum qui in redemptionem nostram venturus est verum credo fideliter Christum.
chunks.english: Well does Jacob, in that same place, suddenly turn to speak in the voice of the elect, saying in verse eighteen: 'I will await your salvation, Lord'—that is, not like the faithless awaiting Antichrist, but I faithfully believe that the one who is to come for our redemption is the true Christ.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Salomon in Proverbiis ait: Et qui stultus apparuit, postquam in sublime elevatus est. Si enim intellexisset, ori imposuisset manum (Prov. XXX, 32; Moral., lib. XXX, c. 3, n. 10). Antichristus quippe in sublime elevabitur, cum Deum se esse mentietur. Sed elevatus in sublime stultus apparebit, quia in ipsa elatione sua per adventum veri Judicis deficiet. Quod si intellexisset, ori imposuisset manum, id est, si supplicium suum, cum superbire exorsus est, praevidisset, bene aliquando conditus, in tanta jactatione superbiae non fuisset elatus.
chunks.english: Solomon says in the book of Proverbs, chapter thirty, verse thirty-two: 'He who appeared foolish after he was elevated on high—if indeed he had understood, he would have put his hand over his mouth.' For Antichrist will be elevated on high when he lies that he is God. But elevated on high he will appear foolish, because in that very exaltation he will fail at the coming of the true Judge. But if he had understood, he would have put his hand over his mouth—that is, if he had foreseen his punishment when he began to be proud, he who was once well hidden would not have been exalted in such boasting of pride.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In fallacia sua Antichristus gradietur, sed juxta breve tempus vitae praesentis ipsa sibi fallacia prosperabitur. Unde per Danielem prophetam dictum est: Robur datum est ei contra juge sacrificium, propter peccata, et prosternetur veritas in terra, et faciet et prosperabitur (Dan. VIII, 12). Quod Salomon ait: Incedit feliciter (Prov. XXX, 29; Moral., lib. XIV, c. 23, n. 27), hoc Daniel dicit: Faciet, prosperabitur.
chunks.english: Antichrist will walk in his deceit, but according to the brief time of the present life, that very deceit will prosper for him. Hence it is said through the prophet Daniel, chapter eight, verse twelve: 'Strength was given to him against the continual sacrifice, because of sins, and truth will be cast down to the ground, and he will act and prosper.' What Solomon says in Proverbs, chapter thirty, verse twenty-nine, 'He walks prosperously,' this Daniel says: 'He will act and prosper.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Scriptum quippe est de Antichristi temporibus: In diebus ejus stupebunt novissimi, et primos invadet horror (Job. XVIII, 20). Tanta enim tunc contra justos iniquitate effrenabitur, ut etiam electorum corda non parvo pavore feriantur. Ita ut in errorem inducantur, si fieri potest, etiam electi (Matth. XXIV, 24). Quod videlicet dicitur, non quia electi casuri sunt, sed magnis terroribus trepidaturi.
chunks.english: For it is written of Antichrist's times in the book of Job, chapter eighteen, verse twenty: 'In his days the last ones will be stunned, and horror will invade the first.' For then he will be so unbridled in iniquity against the just that even the hearts of the elect will be struck with no small fear—as the Gospel of Matthew, chapter twenty-four, verse twenty-four says: 'So as to lead into error, if it were possible, even the elect.' This is said not because the elect will fall, but because they will tremble with great terrors.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tunc contra Antichristum certamen justitiae et novissimi electi habere narrantur et primi, quia scilicet et hi qui in fine mundi electi reperientur in morte carnis prosternendi sunt, et illi etiam qui a prioribus mundi partibus processerunt, Enoch scilicet et Elias, ad medium revocabuntur, et crudelitatis ejus saevitiam in sua adhuc mortali carne passuri sunt.
chunks.english: Then both the last and the first of the elect are said to have the contest of justice against Antichrist, because both those who will be found elect at the world's end must be laid low in the death of the flesh, and those also who proceeded from the earlier parts of the world—namely Enoch and Elijah—will be called back to the middle, and will suffer the savagery of his cruelty still in their mortal flesh.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Antiquus hostis viribus suis traditus, pauco quidem in tempore, sed in multa contra nos virtute, laxatur (Moral., lib. XXXII, c. 15, n. 22, 23). Quem quia vis saevitiae ad crudelitatem dilatat, superna tamen misericordia dierum brevitate coangustat. Hinc enim per semetipsam Veritas dicit: Erit tunc tribulatio magna, qualis non fuit ab initio mundi usque modo, neque fiet (Matth. XXIV, 21). Hinc rursum ait: Nisi breviati fuissent dies illi, non fieret salva omnis caro (Ib., 22).
chunks.english: The ancient enemy, given over to his own powers, is loosed for a short time indeed, but with much strength against us. Because the force of savagery expands him toward cruelty, yet heavenly mercy constrains him by the brevity of days. For Truth itself says in the Gospel of Matthew, chapter twenty-four, verse twenty-one: 'Then there will be great tribulation, such as has not been from the beginning of the world until now, nor ever will be.' And again it says in verse twenty-two: 'Unless those days had been shortened, no flesh would be saved.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quia enim et superbos nos et infirmos Dominus conspicit, dies, quos singulariter malos intulit, misericorditer breviatos dicit (Moral. XXXII, c. 15, n. 27). Profecto ut et superbiam terreat de adversitate temporis, et infirmitatem refoveat de brevitate.
chunks.english: Because the Lord sees us both proud and weak, he mercifully calls shortened the days he brought forth as singularly evil—certainly so that he might terrify pride by the adversity of the time, and comfort weakness by its brevity.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Daniel Propheta, de Antichristi temporibus loquens, ait: Considerabam cornua decem, et ecce cornu aliud parvulum ortum est de medio eorum, et tria de cornibus primis evulsa sunt a facie ejus: et ecce oculi quasi oculi hominis erant in cornu isto, et os loquens ingentia (Dan. VII, 8). Quia enim per undenarium numerum transgressio exprimitur, hujus bestiae cornu undecimo ipse auctor transgressionis indicatur.
chunks.english: The prophet Daniel, speaking of Antichrist's times, says in chapter seven, verse eight: 'I was considering the ten horns, and behold, another little horn sprang up from among them, and three of the first horns were plucked up before it; and behold, eyes like the eyes of a man were in this horn, and a mouth speaking great things.' Because transgression is expressed by the number eleven, the author of transgression himself is indicated by the eleventh horn of this beast.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quod videlicet parvulum oritur, quia purus homo generatur: sed immaniter crescit, quia usque ad conjunctam sibi vim Angelicae fortitudinis proficit. Damnatus ille homo in fine mundi tria cornua, quae ante faciem sunt, evellit, quia ditioni suae regna totidem, quae sibi vicina sunt, subigit.
chunks.english: It rises small because a mere man is generated, but it grows monstrously, because it advances even to the angelic strength joined to it. That damned man at the world's end will pluck up three horns that are before his face, because he will subjugate to his dominion three kingdoms that are near to him.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cujus oculi sunt ut oculi hominis, sed os ingentia loquitur; quia in illo humana quidem forma cernitur, sed verbis suis ultra homines elevatur. Quod per Paulum Apostolum dicitur: Extollens se supra omne quod dicitur Deus, aut quod colitur (II Thess. II, 4), hoc Daniel propheta testatur dicens: Os loquens ingentia.
chunks.english: His eyes are like the eyes of a man, but his mouth speaks great things, because in him human form is indeed seen, but by his words he is elevated beyond men. What is said through Paul the Apostle in his second letter to the Thessalonians, chapter two, verse four—'Exalting himself above all that is called God or that is worshiped'—this the prophet Daniel testifies, saying: 'A mouth speaking great things.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quod vero illum vel Daniel ingentia eloqui, vel Paulus perhibet supra cultum deitatis extolli, hoc est, quod a beato Job divino eloquio cedro comparatur (Job XL, 12). More enim cedri quasi ad alta nititur, dum in omni fastu fallaciae, et virtutis robore, et culminis elatione prosperatur.
chunks.english: That Daniel reports he speaks great things, and Paul testifies he is exalted above the worship of deity—this is what blessed Job compares to a cedar in divine discourse, chapter forty, verse twelve. For in the manner of a cedar he strives toward the heights, while he prospers in all the pomp of deceit, both in the strength of power and in the exaltation of his summit.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXVII. De Antichristo vel ejus membris.
chunks.english: Chapter 27: Antichrist and His Members

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Fortitudo Antichristi non suis viribus roboratur, quia Satanae virtute in gloriam perditionis attollitur, sicut scriptum est: Consurget rex impudens facie, et intelligens propositiones, et roborabitur fortitudo ejus, sed non in viribus suis (Dan. VIII, 23; Moral. lib. XXXII, c. 15, n. 26).
chunks.english: The strength of Antichrist is not strengthened by his own powers, because he is raised up to the glory of perdition by Satan's power, as it is written in the prophet Daniel, chapter eight, verse twenty-three: 'A king shall arise, shameless in face and understanding dark schemes, and his strength shall be made strong, but not by his own powers.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Daniel propheta de eodem Antichristo ait: Dejecit de fortitudine, et de stellis, et conculcavit eas, et usque ad Principem fortitudinis magnificatus est, et ab eo abstulit juge sacrificium et dejecit locum sanctificationis ejus. Robur autem datum ei est contra juge sacrificium propter peccata; et prosternetur veritas in terra (Ibid. 10, 11, 12).
chunks.english: The prophet Daniel says of the same Antichrist in verses ten through twelve: 'He cast down some of the host and some of the stars, and trampled them, and he magnified himself even up to the Prince of the host, and from him he took away the continual sacrifice and cast down the place of his sanctuary. And strength was given to him against the continual sacrifice because of sins, and truth will be cast down to the ground.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ille principaliter damnatus homo est, qui alarum cymbalum vocatur (Isai. XVIII, 1; Moral. lib. XIII, c. 10, n. 13), quia hi qui per superbiam in altitudinem cogitationis evolant eumdem perversum hominem praedicando sonant. Quae scilicet terra recte trans flumina Aethiopiae esse perhibetur. Aethiopia etenim nigrum populum mittit; et omnem hominem mundus iste, quia peccatorem profert, quasi nigrum Aethiopia populum parit.
chunks.english: That principally damned man is the one called the whirring of wings, as Isaiah chapter eighteen, verse one says, because those who fly through pride into the height of thought sound forth by preaching that same perverse man. This land is rightly said to be beyond the rivers of Ethiopia. For Ethiopia sends forth a black people, and this world brings forth every man as a sinner, just as Ethiopia produces a black people.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed terra cui vae dicitur trans flumina Aethiopiae esse perhibetur, quia damnatus ille homo tanta immensitate iniquus est, ut omnium peccantium peccata transcendat. Perversus ille homo in mari legatos mittit, quia ipse praedicatores suos in saeculum spargit. De quibus recte subditur qualiter mittantur, dum dicitur: In vasis papyri super aquas (Isai. XVIII, 1). Ex papyro quippe charta est. Quid itaque per papyrum nisi saecularis scientia designatur? Vasa ergo papyri sunt corda doctorum saecularium.
chunks.english: But the land to which woe is said is reported to be beyond the rivers of Ethiopia, because that damned man is so immensely wicked that he transcends the sins of all who sin. That perverse man sends envoys on the sea, because he scatters his preachers throughout the world. Concerning them it is rightly added how they are sent, when it says in Isaiah chapter eighteen, verse one: 'In vessels of papyrus on the waters.' For from papyrus comes parchment. What therefore is designated by papyrus but secular learning? Therefore vessels of papyrus are the hearts of secular teachers.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In Apocalypsi per Joannem dicitur: Vidi aliam bestiam ascendentem de terra (Apoc. XIII, 11; Moral. lib. XXXIII, c. 35, n. 59). Priorem quippe bestiam, id est, Antichristum, superiori descriptione narraverat. Post quem haec alia bestia ascendisse dicitur, quia post eum multitudo praedicatorum illius ex terrena potestate crassatur. De terra quippe ascendere est de terrena gloria superbire.
chunks.english: In the book of Revelation, John says in chapter thirteen, verse eleven: 'I saw another beast ascending from the earth.' For he had narrated the first beast—that is, Antichrist—in the earlier description. After him this other beast is said to have ascended, because after him the multitude of his preachers is made thick from earthly power. For to ascend from the earth is to be proud of earthly glory.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quae bestia habet duo cornua agni similia, quia per hypocrisim sanctitatis eam quam in se veraciter Dominus habuit singularem sibi inesse et sapientiam mentitur et vitam. Sed quia sub agni specie auditoribus reprobis serpentinum virus infunditur, recte illic subditur: Et loquebatur ut draco (Apoc. XIII, 11). Ista ergo bestia, id est praedicantium multitudo, si aperte ut draco loqueretur, agno similis non appareret; sed assumit agni speciem, ut draconis exerceat operationem.
chunks.english: This beast has two horns like a lamb, because through the hypocrisy of holiness it falsely claims to possess in itself the unique wisdom and life that the Lord truly had. But because under the appearance of a lamb, serpentine poison is poured into reprobate hearers, it is rightly added there in verse eleven: 'And it spoke like a dragon.' Therefore this beast—that is, the multitude of preachers—if it spoke openly like a dragon, would not appear like a lamb; but it assumes the appearance of a lamb in order to carry out the operation of a dragon.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Amaritudo perversarum mentium in hoc tempore non erumpit exterius in vocem pravae libertatis: veniet profecto tempus quando perversi atque carnales aperta voce praedicent quod nunc occulta cogitatione moliuntur (Moral., lib. XIX, c. 9, n. 15). Veniet tempus quando catholicam Ecclesiam non solum injustis vocibus, sed etiam crudelibus plagis premant.
chunks.english: The bitterness of perverse minds does not in this time break out externally into the voice of depraved license. The time will certainly come when the perverse and carnal will preach with open voice what they now contrive in hidden thought. The time will come when they will oppress the Catholic Church not only with unjust voices but also with cruel blows.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In Evangelio Veritas dicit: Surgent pseudo-christi et pseudo-prophetae, et dabunt signa magna et prodigia, ita ut in errorem mittantur, si fieri potest, etiam electi (Matth. XXIV, 24). Nunc fideles nostri mira faciunt, cum perversa patiuntur; tunc autem Antichristi satellites, etiam cum perversa inferunt, mira facturi sunt (Moral. lib. XXXII, c. 15, n. 24).
chunks.english: In the Gospel, Truth says in Matthew chapter twenty-four, verse twenty-four: 'False christs and false prophets will arise, and will give great signs and wonders, so as to lead into error, if it were possible, even the elect.' Now our faithful ones work wonders when they suffer perverse things; but then the satellites of Antichrist will work wonders even as they inflict perverse things.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Pensamus quae erit humanae mentis illa tentatio temporibus Antichristi, quando pius martyr et corpus tormentis subjicit, et tamen ante ejus oculos miracula tortor facit. Cujus tunc virtus non ab ipso cogitationum fundo quatiatur, quando his qui flagris cruciat signis coruscat? Quia nimirum et altus tunc erit veneratione prodigii, et durus crudelitate tormenti. Non enim sola tunc potestate erigitur, sed etiam signorum ostensione fulcitur (N. 25).
chunks.english: Let us consider what will be that temptation of the human mind in the times of Antichrist, when the devout martyr both subjects his body to torments, and yet before his eyes the torturer works miracles. Whose virtue will not then be shaken from the very depth of thoughts, when he who tortures with scourges flashes with signs? Because certainly he will then be exalted by veneration of the prodigy, and harsh by the cruelty of the torment. For he will not then be raised up by power alone, but will also be supported by the display of signs.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Surgent, inquit, pseudo-christi et pseudo-prophetae et dabunt signa magna et prodigia, ita ut in errorem inducantur, si fieri potest, etiam electi. Qua in re valde quaerendum est quomodo aut hi qui electi sunt induci in errorem possint, aut cur si fieri potest, quasi ex dubietate subditur, cum quid faciendum sit Dominus omnia praesciens praestolatur (Moral. XXXIII, c. 36, n. 61).
chunks.english: He says: 'False christs and false prophets will arise and will give great signs and wonders, so as to lead into error, if it were possible, even the elect.' In this matter it must be greatly asked how those who are elect can be led into error, or why 'if it were possible' is added as if from doubt, when the Lord who foreknows all things awaits what is to be done.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Electorum cor temporibus Antichristi et trepida cogitatione concutietur, et tamen eorum constantia non movebitur. Una hac sententia Dominus utrumque complexus est, dicens: Ita ut in errorem inducantur, si fieri potest, etiam electi. Quasi enim jam errare est in cogitatione titubare; sed protinus si fieri potest subjungitur, quia procul dubio fieri non potest ut in errore plene electi capiantur.
chunks.english: The heart of the elect in the times of Antichrist will be shaken by trembling thought, yet their constancy will not be moved. With this one sentence the Lord embraced both, saying: 'So as to lead into error, if it were possible, even the elect.' For to waver in thought is as if already to err; but immediately 'if it were possible' is added, because without doubt it cannot happen that the elect be fully caught in error.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXVIII. De secundo adventu Domini nostri Jesu Christi.
chunks.english: Chapter 28: The Second Coming of Christ

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Mediator Dei et hominum homo Christus Jesus sicut humilis venit ad passionem, ita in extremo judicio terribilis apparebit in reproborum ultione. Ipse Dominus in Evangelio ait: Virtutes coelorum commovebuntur, et tunc videbunt Filium hominis venientem in nubibus in potestate magna et majestate (Luc. XXI, 26, 27; Lib. I in Ev., hom. 1, n. 2).
chunks.english: The Mediator between God and humanity, the man Christ Jesus, just as he came humbly to his passion, so will he appear terrible in the final judgment in the vengeance upon the reprobate. The Lord himself says in the Gospel of Luke, chapter twenty-one, verses twenty-six and twenty-seven: 'The powers of heaven will be shaken, and then they will see the Son of Man coming in clouds with great power and majesty.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid enim Dominus virtutes coelorum, nisi angelos, archangelos, thronos, dominationes, principatus et potestates, appellat? Quae angelorum multitudo in adventu districti Judicis nostris tunc oculis visibiliter apparebunt, ut stricte tunc a nobis exigant hoc quod nos modo invisibilis conditor aequanimiter portat. In potestate et majestate Christum visuri sunt, quem in humilitate positum audire noluerunt, ut virtutem ejus tanto tunc districtius sentiant, quanto nunc cervicem cordis ad ejus patientiam non inclinant.
chunks.english: For what does the Lord call the powers of heaven but angels, archangels, thrones, dominations, principalities, and powers? This multitude of angels will appear visibly to our eyes at the coming of the strict Judge, so that they may then strictly demand from us what the invisible Creator now bears with us equably. They will see Christ in power and majesty who refused to hear him when he was placed in humility, so that they may then feel his power all the more strictly as they now do not incline the neck of their heart to his patience.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Isaias propheta ait: Dominus ad judicium veniet cum senioribus populi sui et principibus ejus (Isai. III, 14; Moral. XXVI, c. 27, n. 51). Cum eis Christus ad judicium veniet, de quibus ipse in Evangelio dicit: Vos, qui secuti estis me, in regeneratione, cum sederit Filius hominis in sede majestatis suae, sedebitis et vos super duodecim sedes, judicantes duodecim tribus Israel (Matth. XIX, 28).
chunks.english: The prophet Isaiah says in chapter three, verse fourteen: 'The Lord will come to judgment with the elders of his people and its princes.' Christ will come to judgment with those of whom he himself says in the Gospel of Matthew, chapter nineteen, verse twenty-eight: 'You who have followed me, in the regeneration, when the Son of Man sits on the throne of his majesty, you also will sit on twelve thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hoc judicium, cum ad retributionem sanctae Ecclesiae Salomon propinquare conspiceret, dixit: Nobilis in portis vir ejus, quando sederit cum senatoribus terrae (Prov. XXXI, 23). Ac si aperte dicat: Quia ultimi judicii sententiam cum sanctis ejusdem Ecclesiae praedicatoribus decernit.
chunks.english: When Solomon saw this judgment approaching for the reward of the holy Church, he said in the book of Proverbs, chapter thirty-one, verse twenty-three: 'Her husband is noble in the gates, when he sits with the senators of the land.' As if saying openly: Because he pronounces the sentence of the final judgment with the holy preachers of that same Church.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXIX. De resurrectione mortuorum.
chunks.english: Chapter 29: The Resurrection of the Dead

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sunt nonnulli qui de resurrectione carnis incerti sunt, et dum carnem in putredinem ossaque in pulverem redigi per sepulcra conspiciunt, reparari ex pulvere carnem et ossa diffidunt; sicque apud se quasi ratiocinantes dicunt: Quando ex pulvere homo reducitur? quando agitur, ut cinis animetur (Lib. II in Ev., homil. 26, n. 12)?
chunks.english: There are some who are uncertain about the resurrection of the flesh, and when they see flesh reduced to rot and bones to dust through the graves, they doubt that flesh and bones can be restored from dust. And so, reasoning among themselves, they say: When is man brought back from dust? When does it happen that ash is animated?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Longe minus est Deo reparare quod fuit, quam creasse quod non fuit. Aut quid mirum, si hominem ex pulvere resuscitet, qui simul omnia ex nihilo creavit? mirabilius est Deo coelum ac terram ex nullis existentibus rebus condidisse, quam ipsum hominem ex terra reparare.
chunks.english: It is far less for God to restore what has been than to create what has not been. Or what wonder is it if he raises man from dust, who created all things at once from nothing? It is more wonderful for God to have established heaven and earth from things that did not exist than to restore man himself from earth.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hominis mortui cinis in sepulcro attenditur, et in carnem redire posse desperatur, et divinae operationis virtus comprehendi quasi ex ratione quaeritur. Quid mirum est, si ossa, nervos, carnem capillosque, Deus reducat ex pulvere, qui lignum, fructus, folia in magna mole arboris ex parvo quotidie semine restaurat?
chunks.english: The ash of a dead man is observed in the grave, and it is despaired that it can return to flesh, and the power of divine operation is sought to be comprehended as if by reason. What wonder is it if God restores bones, sinews, flesh, and hair from dust, who daily restores wood, fruits, and leaves in the great mass of a tree from a small seed?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum ergo dubitans animus de resurrectionis potentia rationem quaerit, earumdem rerum ei quaestiones inferendae sunt, quae et incessanter fiunt, et tamen ratione comprehendi nequaquam possunt. Ut dum non valet ex visione rei penetrare quod conspicit, de promissione divinae potentiae credat quod audit.
chunks.english: When therefore the doubting mind seeks a rational explanation for the power of resurrection, questions must be brought to it about those very things that are done continually yet cannot at all be comprehended by reason—so that while it cannot penetrate by seeing what it beholds, it may believe from the promise of divine power what it hears.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: David psalmista ait: Exsurge, Domine, in requiem tuam, tu et arca sanctificationis tuae (Psal. CXXXI, 8; lib. II in Ezech., homil. 8, n. 6). Exsurrexit enim Dominus in requiem suam, cum carnem suam de sepulcro suscitavit. Post hunc quoque exsurgit et arca, quia resurgit Ecclesia.
chunks.english: David the psalmist says in Psalm one hundred thirty-one, verse eight: 'Arise, Lord, into your rest, you and the ark of your sanctification.' For the Lord arose into his rest when he raised his flesh from the tomb. After him the ark also arises, because the Church rises.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per Ezechielem prophetam dicitur: Ossa arida, audite verbum Domini. Haec dicit Dominus Deus ossibus his: Ecce ego intromittam in vobis spiritum, et vivetis (Ezech. XVII, 4 seq.). Hinc est quod propheta alius per resurrectionem humanum genus vidit in fine suscitari atque ait: Vivificabit nos post duos dies, in die tertia suscitabit nos, et vivemus in conspectu ejus (Osee, VI, 3).
chunks.english: Through the prophet Ezekiel, chapter seventeen, verses four and following, it says: 'Dry bones, hear the word of the Lord. Thus says the Lord God to these bones: Behold, I will cause spirit to enter into you, and you shall live.' Hence it is that another prophet saw the human race raised up at the end through resurrection and says in the prophet Hosea, chapter six, verse three: 'He will revive us after two days; on the third day he will raise us up, and we shall live in his sight.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum de semetipso Dominus loqueretur, ait: Nolite mirari hoc, quia venit hora in qua omnes qui in monumentis sunt audient vocem ejus. Et procedent qui bona fecerunt in resurrectionem vitae; qui vero mala egerunt, in resurrectionem judicii (Joan. V, 28, 29).
chunks.english: When the Lord spoke about himself, he said in the Gospel of John, chapter five, verses twenty-eight and twenty-nine: 'Do not marvel at this, for the hour is coming in which all who are in the tombs will hear his voice. And they will come forth—those who have done good, to the resurrection of life; but those who have done evil, to the resurrection of judgment.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hinc Paulus ait: Unde etiam Salvatorem exspectamus Dominum Jesum Christum, qui reformabit corpus humilitatis nostrae configuratum corpori claritatis suae (Philip. III, 20). Hinc iterum dicit: Si enim credimus quod Jesus mortuus est, et resurrexit; ita et Deus eos qui dormierunt per Jesum adducet cum eo (I Thess. IV, 14). Qui rursus ait: Primitiae dormientium Christus (I Cor. XV, 20). Si enim nos a mortis somno non resurgimus, quomodo resurrectionem dominicam primitias habemus?
chunks.english: Hence Paul says in his letter to the Philippians, chapter three, verse twenty: 'From which also we await the Savior, the Lord Jesus Christ, who will transform the body of our humility, conformed to the body of his glory.' Hence again he says in his first letter to the Thessalonians, chapter four, verse fourteen: 'For if we believe that Jesus died and rose again, even so God will bring with him those who sleep through Jesus.' Who again says in his first letter to the Corinthians, chapter fifteen, verse twenty: 'Christ the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep.' For if we do not rise from the sleep of death, how do we have the resurrection of the Lord as firstfruits?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ecce veteres ac novi Patres uno sibi spiritu de carnis resurrectione concordant (Lib. II in Ezech., hom. 8, n. 7). Ecce ipsa per se Veritas prius voce docuit, quod de resurrectione carnis postmodum opere demonstravit; et tamen adhuc quorumdam infirmitas fidem non habet, in domo fidei stans.
chunks.english: Behold, the old and new Fathers agree with one spirit about the resurrection of the flesh. Behold, Truth itself first taught by voice what it afterward demonstrated by deed concerning the resurrection of the flesh; and yet still the weakness of some has no faith, though standing in the house of faith.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ipsa nobis elementa, ipsae rerum species resurrectionis imaginem praedicant. Sol enim quotidie nostris oculis moritur, quotidie resurgit. Stellae matutinis horis nobis occidunt, vespere resurgunt. Arbusta aestivis temporibus plena foliis ac fructibus videmus, quae hiemali tempore nuda foliis, floribus, ac fructibus, et quasi arida remanent; sed vernali sole redeunte, cum a radice humor surrexerit, suo iterum decore vestiuntur.
chunks.english: The very elements themselves, the very appearances of things, proclaim to us the image of resurrection. For the sun daily dies to our eyes and daily rises. The stars set for us in the morning hours and rise in the evening. We see trees full of leaves and fruits in summer, which in winter remain bare of leaves, flowers, and fruits, and as if dried up; but when the spring sun returns, when moisture rises from the root, they are clothed again in their beauty.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cur ergo de hominibus diffiditur, quod fieri in lignis videtur? Saepe infidi homines, dum pulverem putrescentis carnis aspiciunt, dicunt: Unde caro et capilli poterunt in resurrectione reparari? Haec itaque requirentes parva semina ingentium arborum videant, atque, si possunt, dicant: Ubi in eis latet tanta moles roboris, tanta diversitas ramorum, tanta multitudo et viriditas foliorum, tanta species florum, tanta ubertas, sapor, atque odor fructuum?
chunks.english: Why then is it doubted concerning men what is seen to happen in trees? Often faithless men, when they look at the dust of rotting flesh, say: From where will flesh and hair be able to be restored in the resurrection? Therefore, seeking these things, let them see the small seeds of huge trees, and if they can, let them say: Where in them is hidden such a mass of strength, such a diversity of branches, such a multitude and greenness of leaves, such an appearance of flowers, such an abundance, taste, and smell of fruits?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nunquidnam semina arborum odorem vel saporem habent quem ipsae post arbores in suis fructibus proferunt? Si ergo ex semine arboris, quod videri non potest, produci potest, cur de pulvere carnis humanae diffiditur quia ex eo reparari forma valeat quae non videtur?
chunks.english: Do the seeds of trees have the smell or taste that the trees themselves afterward put forth in their fruits? If therefore it can be produced from a tree's seed, which cannot be seen, why is it doubted concerning the dust of human flesh that from it can be restored the form which is not seen?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si non potes resurrectionis effectum ratione comprehendere, perpende quanta multa sunt quae non intelligis, qualiter sint, et tamen esse non dubitas (N. 7). Dic, rogo, si nosti gyrum coeli, terrae cardines, aquarum abyssos, ubi finiuntur, ubi suspensi sunt? Scimus autem quia quod ex nihilo factum est pendet in nihilo. Cur de resurrectionis gloria disputando et discutiendo dubitas, qui tam multa mysteria sine discussione credidisti (N. 10)?
chunks.english: If you cannot comprehend the effect of resurrection by reason, consider how many things there are that you do not understand how they are, and yet you do not doubt that they are. Tell me, I ask, if you know the circuit of heaven, the hinges of the earth, the abysses of the waters, where they end, where they are suspended? But we know that what was made from nothing hangs in nothing. Why do you doubt the glory of resurrection by disputing and examining it, you who have believed so many mysteries without examination?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui tamen si resurrectionem carnis non credis, omnia sine causa credidisti, quia in hoc angelorum spectaculo videris quidem velociter currere, sed postquam cursum peregeris, ne bravium accipias, adversaris. Per Paulum apostolum dicitur: Sic currite, ut comprehendatis (I Cor. IX, 24). Qui rursus ait: Si in hac vita in Christo tantum sperantes sumus, miserabiliores sumus omnibus hominibus (I Cor. XV, 19).
chunks.english: Yet if you do not believe in the resurrection of the flesh, you have believed everything for nothing, because in this spectacle of angels you seem indeed to run swiftly, but after you have completed the course, you oppose receiving the prize. Through Paul the apostle it says in his first letter to the Corinthians, chapter nine, verse twenty-four: 'Run in such a way that you may obtain it.' Who again says in chapter fifteen, verse nineteen: 'If in this life only we have hope in Christ, we are of all men most miserable.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Divinae autem virtutis mysteria, quae comprehendi non possunt, non intellectu discutienda sunt, sed fide veneranda. Sciendum nobis est quia quidquid ratione hominis comprehendi potest mirum esse jam non potest, sed sola est in miraculis ratio potentia facientis.
chunks.english: But the mysteries of divine power, which cannot be comprehended, are not to be examined by understanding but venerated by faith. We must know that whatever can be comprehended by human reason can no longer be wonderful, but the only reason in miracles is the power of the one who does them.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sicut citius homo dormiens excitatus a somno vigilat, ita concite ad conditoris nutum a morte corporis resurgit. Praedicator egregius mortem Domini mortem vocat: mortem autem servorum Domini non mortem, sed somnum nominat, quia infirma corda audientium respiciens medicamentum praedicationis suae mira arte componit; et illum, quem jam resurrexisse noverant, eis mortuum insinuare non dubitat (Moral., lib. XII, c. 8, n. 12).
chunks.english: Just as a sleeping man, roused from sleep, awakens more quickly, so he rises swiftly at the Creator's nod from the death of the body. The excellent preacher calls the death of the Lord death, but the death of the Lord's servants he calls not death but sleep, because looking to the weak hearts of his hearers, he composes the medicine of his preaching with wonderful art; and he does not hesitate to suggest to them as dead him whom they knew had already risen.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Eos vero, qui necdum resurrexerant, ut spem resurrectionis insinuet, non mortuos, sed dormientes vocat. Non verebatur Apostolus dicere Christum mortuum, quem auditores jam resurrexisse cognoverant, et verebatur dicere mortuos, quos resurgere vix credebant.
chunks.english: But those who had not yet risen, to suggest the hope of resurrection, he calls not dead but sleeping. The Apostle was not afraid to say Christ was dead, whom his hearers knew had already risen, and he was afraid to say dead those whom they scarcely believed would rise.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In illa resurrectionis gloria erit corpus nostrum subtile quidem per effectum spiritualis potentiae, sed palpabile per veritatem naturae. De vera corporis resurrectione beatus Job loquens, ait: Rursum circumdabor pelle mea, et in carne mea videbo Deum, quem visurus sum ego ipse, et oculi mei conspecturi sunt, et non alius (Job, XIX, 26; Moral., lib. XIV, c. 56, n. 72, 76).
chunks.english: In that glory of resurrection our body will be subtle indeed through the effect of spiritual power, but palpable through the truth of nature. Blessed Job, speaking of the true resurrection of the body, says in chapter nineteen, verse twenty-six: 'Again I shall be clothed with my skin, and in my flesh I shall see God, whom I myself shall behold, and my eyes shall look upon, and not another.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ecce veram resurrectionem: ecce pellem, ecce carnem apertis vocibus fatetur. Quid ergo remanet, unde possit mens nostra dubitare? Nos autem beati Job fidem sequentes, et Redemptoris nostri post resurrectionem corpus palpabile veraciter credentes, fatemur carnem nostram post resurrectionem futuram et eamdem et diversam: eamdem per naturam, diversam per potentiam (Cap. 57, n. 77).
chunks.english: Behold the true resurrection: behold he confesses with open words skin, behold flesh. What then remains by which our mind can doubt? But we, following the faith of blessed Job, and truly believing that our Redeemer's body after resurrection was palpable, confess that our flesh after resurrection will be both the same and different: the same by nature, different by power.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Erit post resurrectionem caro nostra subtilis, erit palpabilis, quia non amittet essentiam veracis naturae. In terra videlicet mortui, non spiritu, sed corpore descendunt (Lib. II in Ezech., homil. 8, n. 6). In conspectu ergo Domini procedunt, qui in terram descendunt, quia resurgendo ad judicium veniunt, qui nunc in pulvere putrescunt, sicut scriptum est: In conspectu ejus procedent universi qui descendunt in terram (Psal. XXI, 29).
chunks.english: After resurrection our flesh will be subtle, it will be palpable, because it will not lose the essence of true nature. In the earth the dead descend, not in spirit but in body. Therefore they proceed in the sight of the Lord who descend into the earth, because they come to judgment by rising, who now rot in dust, as it is written in the Psalms, chapter twenty-one, verse twenty-nine: 'In his sight shall proceed all who descend into the earth.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Idem Psalmista iterum dicit: Sitivit in te anima mea, quam multipliciter et caro mea (Psal. LXII, 2). Sitit anima, ut Deum videat. Caro, quid sitit, nisi ut resurgat? Hinc rursum ait: Aufer spiritum eorum, et deficient, et in pulverem suum revertentur (Psal. CIII, 29). Moxque de carnis resurrectione subjungit: Emitte spiritum tuum, Deus, et creabuntur, et renovabis faciem terrae (Ibid. 30).
chunks.english: The same psalmist again says in Psalm sixty-two, verse two: 'My soul has thirsted for you, how manifold also has my flesh.' The soul thirsts to see God. The flesh, what does it thirst for but to rise again? Hence again he says in Psalm one hundred three, verse twenty-nine: 'Take away their spirit, and they will fail, and will return to their dust.' And immediately he adds about the resurrection of the flesh in verse thirty: 'Send forth your Spirit, God, and they shall be created, and you shall renew the face of the earth.'

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The Dreadful Judgment of the Eternal King
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXX. De tremendo aeterni Regis judicio.
chunks.english: Chapter 30: The Dreadful Judgment of the Eternal King

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omnipotens Deus Filius, cum Judex mortuorum ac vivorum advenerit, et blandus justis, et terribilis apparebit injustis (Lib. II in Ev., homil. 21, n. 3). Istos in judicium veniens per lenitatis mansuetudinem demulcet. Illos vero per justitiae districtionem terret.
chunks.english: God the Almighty Son, when he comes as Judge of the dead and the living, will appear both gentle to the just and terrible to the unjust. Coming to judgment, he caresses the former through the gentleness of mercy, but terrifies the latter through the strictness of justice.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tempestas cum oritur, prius lenes undae, et postmodum volumina majora concitantur; ad extremum fluctus se in alta erigunt, et navigantes quosque ipsa sua altitudine subvertunt (Moral., lib. XXI, c. 22, n. 35). Sic sic nimirum extrema illa properat, quae universum mundum subruat, tempestas animarum. Tempestas futuri judicii nunc bellis et cladibus, quasi quibusdam undis sua nobis exordia ostendit; et quanto ad finem quotidie propinquiores efficimur, tanto graviora irruere tribulationum volumina videmus.
chunks.english: When a storm arises, first gentle waves, and afterward greater swellings are stirred up; finally the waves raise themselves to the heights, and overturn all navigators by their very height. So, so indeed that final tempest hastens, which will overthrow the entire world, a tempest of souls. The tempest of the coming judgment now shows us its beginnings with wars and calamities, like certain waves; and the closer we become daily to the end, the more we see heavier volumes of tribulations rushing in.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ad extremum vero, commotis omnibus elementis, supernus Judex veniens finem omnium apportat, quia videlicet et tunc tempestas fluctus in coelum levat; unde et dicitur: Adhuc modicum et ego movebo non solum terram, sed etiam coelum (Aggae. II, 7). Quam scilicet tempestatem, quia sancti viri vigilanter aspiciunt, quasi tumentes super se quotidie fluctus expavescunt, atque ex tribulationibus quae mundum feriunt praevident quae sequantur.
chunks.english: But finally, when all elements are shaken, the Judge from above coming brings the end of all, because then indeed the tempest lifts the waves to heaven; whence it is also said in the prophet Haggai, chapter two, verse seven: 'Yet once more, and I will shake not only the earth but also heaven.' This tempest, because holy men watchfully behold it, they tremble daily as if swelling waves were upon them, and from the tribulations that strike the world they foresee what will follow.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid tremendus Judex facturus est, cum per semetipsum venerit, et in ultionem peccatorum ira ejus exarserit, si nunc portari non potest, cum nos per tenuissimam nubem ferit (Lib. I in Evang., homil. 1, n. 6)? In irae ejus praesentia quae caro subsistet, si ventum movit, et terram subruit, concitavit aera et tot aedificia stravit? Hanc districtionem venturi Judicis Paulus considerans, ait: Horrendum est incidere in manus Dei viventis (Hebr. X, 3).
chunks.english: What will the dreadful Judge do when he comes in himself and his wrath burns for the vengeance of sins, if now it cannot be endured when he strikes us through the thinnest cloud? In the presence of his wrath what flesh will stand, if he moved the wind and overthrew the earth, stirred the air and laid low so many buildings? Paul, considering this strictness of the coming Judge, says in his letter to the Hebrews, chapter ten, verse three: 'It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hanc Psalmista exprimit, dicens: Deus manifeste veniet, Deus noster, et non silebit. Ignis in conspectu ejus ardebit, et in circuitu ejus tempestas valida (Psal. XLIX, 3). Districtionem quippe tantae justitiae tempestas ignisque comitantur, quia tempestas examinat quos ignis exurat.
chunks.english: The psalmist expresses this, saying in Psalm forty-nine, verse three: 'God shall come manifestly, our God, and shall not be silent. Fire shall burn before him, and round about him a mighty tempest.' For the tempest and fire accompany the strictness of such justice, because the tempest examines those whom the fire will consume.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per Sophoniam prophetam dicitur: Juxta est dies Domini magnus, et velox nimis. Vox diei Domini amara, tribulabitur ibi fortis. Dies irae, dies illa, dies tribulationis et angustiae, dies calamitatis et miseriae, dies tenebrarum et caliginis, dies nebulae et turbinis, dies tubae et clangoris super omnes civitates munitas, et super omnes angulos excelsos (Sophon. I, 14 seq.).
chunks.english: Through the prophet Zephaniah, chapter one, verses fourteen and following, it says: 'Near is the great day of the Lord, and exceedingly swift. The voice of the day of the Lord is bitter; the strong man shall be troubled there. That day is a day of wrath, a day of tribulation and distress, a day of calamity and misery, a day of darkness and obscurity, a day of clouds and whirlwind, a day of trumpet and alarm over all fortified cities and over all lofty corners.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dies Domini vindictae, atque animadversionis plena super civitates munitas et super angulos excelsos venit, quia ira extremi judicii humana corda et defensionibus contra veritatem clausa destruit, et duplicitatibus involuta dissolvit (Reg. Past., part. III, c. 12). Tunc munitae civitates cadunt, quia mentes Deo impenetratae damnabuntur. Tunc excelsi anguli corruunt, quia corda quae se per impuritatis prudentiam erigunt per justitiae sententiam prosternuntur.
chunks.english: The day of the Lord full of vengeance and punishment comes upon fortified cities and upon lofty corners, because the wrath of the final judgment destroys human hearts both closed by defenses against truth and dissolves them wrapped in duplicities. Then fortified cities fall, because minds impenetrable to God will be condemned. Then lofty corners collapse, because hearts that raise themselves through the prudence of impurity are cast down by the sentence of justice.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: De hac die judicii Dominus iterum per prophetam dicit: Adhuc semel, et ego movebo non solum terram, sed etiam et coelum (Aggae. II, 22; Lib, I in Evang., homil. 1, n. 9). Si cum aerem movit, et terra non subsistit, quis ferat, cum coelum moverit? Terrores quos modo cernimus sequentis irae praecones sunt.
chunks.english: Concerning this day of judgment the Lord again says through the prophet Haggai, chapter two, verse twenty-two: 'Yet once more, and I will shake not only the earth but also heaven.' If when he moved the air, the earth does not stand, who will endure when he moves heaven? The terrors we now see are heralds of the coming wrath.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde considerare necesse est quia ab illa tribulatione ultima tantum sunt tribulationes istae dissimiles, quantum a potentia judicis persona praeconis distat. Illum ergo terribilem diem tota intentione cogitemus, vitam moresque corrigamus, mala tentantia resistendo vincamus, perpetrata autem fletibus puniamus. Adventum namque aeterni Judicis tanto securiores quandoque videbimus, quanto nunc districtionem illius timendo praevenimus.
chunks.english: Hence it is necessary to consider that these tribulations are as different from that final tribulation as the person of the herald is distant from the power of the judge. Therefore let us ponder that terrible day with all intentness, let us correct our life and conduct, let us conquer tempting evils by resisting, and let us punish those perpetrated with tears. For we will someday see the coming of the eternal Judge all the more securely as we now anticipate his strictness by fearing it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In tremendi Judicis examine electi omnes a reprobis divisi gaudebunt, quia cum iniquos omnes extrema ultio percutit, ipsi de gloria dignae retributionis hilarescunt (Moral. VI, c. 30, n. 48). In aeterna vita justi constituti damnatis jam tunc ex humanitate non compatiuntur, quia divinae justitiae per speciem inhaerentes inconcusso districtionis intimae vigore solidantur.
chunks.english: In the examination of the dreadful Judge, all the elect, separated from the reprobate, will rejoice, because when final vengeance strikes all the wicked, they themselves rejoice in the glory of worthy retribution. The just, established in eternal life, do not then sympathize from humanity with the damned, because cleaving to divine justice through vision, they are made solid by the unshaken vigor of inner strictness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Redemptori generis humani ad aedificationem nostram minime sufficit quod in extremo judicio dicturum se esse perhibuit: Quandiu fecistis uni ex his fratribus meis minimis, mihi fecistis (Matth. XXV, 40; Lib. II in Evang., homil. 39, n. 10), nisi et ante judicium hoc in se ostenderet quod dixisset, ut videlicet demonstraret quia bona quisquis nunc opera indigentibus exhibet, ei haec specialiter impendit, cujus haec amore exhibuerit.
chunks.english: It is hardly sufficient for the Redeemer of the human race for our edification that he declared he would say in the final judgment, as the Gospel of Matthew, chapter twenty-five, verse forty records: 'As long as you did it to one of these my least brothers, you did it to me'—unless he also showed in himself before judgment what he had said, so that he might demonstrate that whoever now performs good works for the needy, renders them especially to him, for love of whom he has performed them.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXI. De poenitentia reproborum sine fructu.
chunks.english: Chapter 31: Fruitless Repentance of the Reprobate

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Aeterno Judice veniente, unusquisque reproborum ad cor suum reducitur, ut sancta jam cum agere non valet, sentiat, et rectum quod sequi debuit ex tortitudinis suae poena cognoscat (Moral., lib. XXVII, c. 24, n, 45). Per Jeremiam prophetam dicitur: Congrega eos quasi gregem ad victimam, et sanctifica eos in die occisionis (Jerem. XII, 3). In die enim occisionis sanctificantur reprobi, quia tunc sancta quae debuerunt agere sentiunt, cum digna pravitatis suae supplicia jam declinare non possunt.
chunks.english: When the eternal Judge comes, each of the reprobate is brought back to his heart, so that when he can no longer do holy things, he may feel it, and may know from the punishment of his perversity the right way he should have followed. Through the prophet Jeremiah, chapter twelve, verse three, it says: 'Gather them like a flock for sacrifice, and sanctify them in the day of slaughter.' For on the day of slaughter the reprobate are sanctified, because then they feel the holy things they should have done, when they can no longer avoid the punishments worthy of their wickedness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Reprobi omnes tunc feriri pertimescunt, cum ultione extrema per sententiam Judicis feriuntur, eosque tunc terror ejus a somno sui torporis exsuscitat, cum vindicta perturbat (Moral. XI, c. 29, n. 41). Per prophetam dicitur: Et tantum sola vexatio intellectum dabit auditui (Isai. XXVIII, 19). Cum enim de praeceptis Dei contemptis atque despectis verberari per vindictam iniqui coeperint, tunc intelligunt quod audierunt.
chunks.english: All the reprobate then fear to be struck when they are struck by the final vengeance through the sentence of the Judge, and his terror then awakens them from the sleep of their torpor when vengeance disturbs them. Through the prophet Isaiah, chapter twenty-eight, verse nineteen, it says: 'And vexation alone will give understanding to the hearing.' For when the wicked begin to be scourged through vengeance for the commandments of God that were despised and rejected, then they understand what they heard.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et Psalmista ait: Cum occideret eos, tunc quaerebant eum (Psal. LXXVII, 34). Omnes impii in die extremi judicii cognita sua damnatione dicturi sunt: Erravimus a via veritatis, et lumen justitiae non luxit nobis, et sol non ortus est nobis (Sap. V, 6; Moral. XXXIV, c. 14, n. 25). Ac si aperte dicant: Interni nobis luminis radius non refulsit.
chunks.english: And the psalmist says in Psalm seventy-seven, verse thirty-four: 'When he slew them, then they sought him.' All the wicked on the day of final judgment, having known their damnation, will say, as the book of Wisdom, chapter five, verse six records: 'We have erred from the way of truth, and the light of justice has not shone for us, and the sun has not risen upon us.' As if saying openly: The ray of inner light has not shone back to us.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nescit impius mala quae fecit, nisi cum pro eisdem malis puniri jam coeperit (Moral. XV, c. 51, n. 58). Tunc namque intelligit quod audivit, cum se jam pro contemptu vexari doluerit, sicut scriptum est: Et tantummodo sola vexatio intellectum dabit auditui (Isai. XXVIII, 19; Moral. VII, c. 27, n. 33). Reprobi aeterna non intelligunt, nisi cum temporalibus jam sine emendatione puniuntur.
chunks.english: The wicked man does not know the evils he has done unless he has already begun to be punished for those same evils. For then he understands what he heard, when he has already grieved to be vexed for his contempt, as it is written: 'And vexation alone will give understanding to the hearing.' The reprobate do not understand eternal things unless they are already punished with temporal things without amendment.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tunc namque mens aestuat, et infructuosae poenitentiae se ignibus inflammat, duci ad supplicium timet, praesentem vitam ex desiderio retinet; sed de loco suo solvitur, quia oblectamenta carnis deserens, ejus duritia per supplicium liquatur.
chunks.english: For then the mind burns, and inflames itself with the fires of fruitless repentance; it fears to be led to punishment, it holds onto the present life out of desire; but it is dissolved from its place, because abandoning the delights of the flesh, its hardness is melted through punishment.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Damnation of the Devil and Demons
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXII. De damnatione diaboli vel daemonum.
chunks.english: Chapter 32: Damnation of the Devil and Demons

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Aeterno Judice terribiliter apparente, astantibus legionibus angelorum, assistente cuncto ministerio coelestium potestatum, atque electis omnibus ad hoc spectaculum deductis, diabolus crudelis et fortis bellua in medium captiva deducitur, et cum suo corpore, id est, cum reprobis omnibus aeternis gehennae incendiis mancipatur, cum dicitur: Discedite a me, maledicti, in ignem aeternum, qui praeparatus est diabolo et angelis ejus (Matth. XXV, 41; Moral. XXXIII, c. 20, n. 37).
chunks.english: When the eternal Judge appears terribly, with legions of angels standing by, with all the ministry of heavenly powers assisting, and with all the elect brought to this spectacle, the devil—cruel and strong beast—is led captive into the midst, and with his body, that is, with all the reprobate, is consigned to the eternal fires of hell, when it is said in the Gospel of Matthew, chapter twenty-five, verse forty-one: 'Depart from me, you cursed, into the eternal fire that has been prepared for the devil and his angels.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: O quale erit illud spectaculum, quando haec immanissima bestia electorum oculis ostendetur, quae hoc belli tempore nimis illos terrere potuerat, si videretur! Sed occulto ac miro Dei consilio agitur ut et nunc per ejus gratiam a pugnantibus non visa vincatur, et tunc a laetis victoribus jam captiva videatur.
chunks.english: Oh what a spectacle it will be when this most monstrous beast is shown to the eyes of the elect, which in this time of war could have terrified them greatly if it had been seen! But it is done by the hidden and wonderful counsel of God that now through his grace it is conquered by the fighters without being seen, and then is seen as already captive by the joyful victors.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tunc justi divino adjutorio quantum debitores sunt plenius recognoscunt, quando tam fortem bestiam viderint, quam nunc infirmi vicerunt. Et in hostis sui immanitate conspiciunt quantum debeant gratiae defensoris sui (Moral. lib. IV, c. 5, n. 10).
chunks.english: Then the just more fully recognize how much they are debtors to divine help, when they will have seen so strong a beast, which they have now conquered while weak. And in the enormity of their enemy they see how much they owe to the grace of their defender.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Antiquus hostis nequitiae suae vinculis astrictus, aliud est quod nunc patitur, aliud quod in fine patietur, quia enim a lucis intimae ordine cecidit, nunc semetipsum intrinsecus erroris caligine confundit. Antiquus hostis damnationis suae tempore amaritudine involvitur, quia ex merito spontaneae caliginis aeterno gehennae tormento cruciatur.
chunks.english: The ancient enemy, bound by the chains of his wickedness, suffers one thing now and another at the end, because since he fell from the order of intimate light, he now confounds himself inwardly with the darkness of error. The ancient enemy at the time of his damnation is wrapped in bitterness, because from the merit of spontaneous darkness he is tortured by the eternal torment of hell.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Recte de Antichristo beatus Job ait: Occupet eum caligo, involvatur amaritudine (Job. III, 5). Omne quippe quod involutum est finem suum quasi nusquam indicat, quia sicut non ostendit quo incipit, ita non detegit quo desinit. Involvi ergo amaritudine antiquus hostis dicitur, quia superbiae ejus supplicia, non solum omnimoda, sed etiam infinita praeparantur.
chunks.english: Rightly blessed Job says of Antichrist in chapter three, verse five: 'Let darkness seize upon him, let him be wrapped in bitterness.' For everything that is wrapped indicates its end nowhere, as it were, because just as it does not show where it begins, so it does not reveal where it ends. Therefore the ancient enemy is said to be wrapped in bitterness, because the punishments of his pride are prepared not only in every way but also infinitely.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Antichristi poena tunc initium accipit, cum districtus ad extremum judicium Judex venit; unde et bene subditur: Noctem illam tenebrosus turbo possideat (Job, III, 6; Moral. IV, c. 6, n. 11). Tenebrosus ergo turbo hanc noctem possidet, quia apostatam spiritum a conspectu districti Judicis ad aeterna supplicia pavenda illa tempestas rapit. Turbine ergo nox ista possidetur, quia superba ejus caecitas districta animadversione percutitur.
chunks.english: The punishment of Antichrist then receives its beginning when the Judge comes strictly to final judgment; whence it is also well added in verse six: 'Let a dark whirlwind possess that night.' Therefore the dark whirlwind possesses this night, because that fearful tempest snatches the apostate spirit from the sight of the strict Judge to eternal punishments. Therefore this night is possessed by the whirlwind, because its proud blindness is struck by strict punishment.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The Eternal Punishments of the Reprobate
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXIII. De aeternis suppliciis reproborum.
chunks.english: Chapter 33: The Eternal Punishments of the Reprobate

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omnipotentis Dei justitia futurorum praescia ab ipsa mundi origine gehennae ignem creavit, qui in poena reproborum esse semel inciperet, sed ardorem suum, etiam sine lignis nunquam finiret (Moral. XV, c. 29, n. 35). Sciendum est quod omnes reprobi qui ex anima simul et carne peccaverunt illic in aeterno supplicio in anima pariter et carne cruciabuntur.
chunks.english: The justice of almighty God, foreknowing the future, created the fire of hell from the very origin of the world—fire that would once begin to exist in the punishment of the reprobate, but would never end its burning, even without wood. It must be known that all the reprobate who sinned with both soul and flesh will there be tortured in eternal punishment equally in soul and flesh.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per David prophetam dicitur: Pones eos ut clibanum ignis in tempore vultus tui (Psal. XX, 10). Clibanus namque intrinsecus ardet. Is vero qui ab igne devoratur ab exteriori incipit parte concremari. Ut ergo sacra eloquia ardere et exterius et interius reprobos demonstrarent, eos et ab igne devorari, et sicut clibanum poni, testantur, ut per ignem crucientur in corpore, et per dolorem ardeant in mente.
chunks.english: Through David the prophet it says in Psalm twenty, verse ten: 'You will make them as an oven of fire in the time of your countenance.' For an oven burns inwardly. But one who is devoured by fire begins to be consumed from the outer part. Therefore, so that the sacred words might demonstrate that the reprobate burn both outwardly and inwardly, they testify that they are both devoured by fire and placed like an oven, so that they are tortured by fire in the body and burn through pain in the mind.
